Content: Total myth making. Stop it. There is no such thing as "AI" if you define "intelligence" appropriately. Read Karen Hao&39;s account of the industry. It is their marketing ploy to say ,"This is the worst nightmare, be afraid!" then out the other side of their mouths, "It is the most utopian tech ever." It&39;s neither. They sow fear, and then say, "Trust us, we&39;re the only ones that can save you from the robot apocalypse." It&39;s pure grift for ponzi profits. Besides that, unemployment is not a productivity gain story, it is an unspent income story, no one seems to understand this, so a lot of fear is entirely misdirected. There is never any government policy excuse for any unemployment (defined as people seeking to exchange their labour for the state tax credits.) Unemployment is a simple epic tragic policy mistake of biblical proportions, worse in terms of lost real output in a decade today than the real losses of all wars in human history. None of it is caused by "technology".

Content: A zero-tolerance for rented software has developed.If you&39;ve used "sold once, used continually" products, you learn that UI complexity capture - where the switching costs of abandoning rented software are planned out - is contrived and unnecessary.The motive of charging, and charging, and charging users for software is understandable. You make more money if you make users keep paying, non-stop, for software.Again, a zero-tolerance for rented software has developed. It is not mere chance that per-seat licensing is unpopular.When you, as a company, adopt a discipline about cutting costs, monthly software fees are the first to get cut.Paying for the same thing over and over is pretty much a scam, and UI/complexity changes only attempt to hide the truth, that we&39;ve allowed some vendors to transfer wealth.I recommend everyone with budget responsibility to take a look at the expense items for your rented software, and remember, originally you would buy the software and own it, not pay for it over, and over, and over.

Content: In an agent driven world where systems interact via MCP, what disappears is the click heavy UI layer, not the core backend. The real value shifts to the structured system of record: canonical data models, permissions, workflows, and auditability. Agents don’t need forms, dashboards, or manual navigation. They operate directly on well defined data and logic through APIs or MCP interfaces. That means your structured data still lives in a reliable backend, typically a relational database like PostgreSQL, while optional layers like vector stores handle unstructured context. The new architecture becomes database + policy engine + workflow runtime + agent interface, with human UI as a thin optional layer. So SaaS is not replaced, it evolves from software humans operate to software that agents operate, with humans supervising exceptions, and the moat shifts from UI to data integrity, schema design, and operational logic.

Content: I was not one to fall for ChatGPT&39;s BS when it just gives me what I want to hear or simply toss guesses or assumptions at me which are dead wrong. Sometimes AI hits the nail on the head, but all too often it&39;s full of crap. I&39;ve even tested it to see if it even knew how to do some simple research on something I was well versed in to see if it either knew or could look up the correct answer regarding some old technology; it got it wrong. I don&39;t care that the personality tells me that I was right to call it out, but I&39;m really unhappy at how damn often it gets shit wrong. For every one question it answers correctly, it will respond with incorrect information the next 10. What&39;s worse is that after it hands me something to try, and it doesn&39;t work, after 3 different responses or so, it goes back to the first suggestion as if it forgot that it suggested it and that it did not work. Not only does AI seem to have a really bad time at also retaining things, but since it&39;s a "learning" model, it can just as easily unlearn as quickly as it learns meaning that it can never be trusted in what it THINKS it knows. I&39;ve yelled at ChatGPT for being so wrong so often and sometimes I don&39;t even have to check what it suggests to me to know it handed me something useless. In the grand scheme of things, AI is junk! Right now I&39;m really angry because of the AI slop shell scripts that I have to review that people could easily study what is already available (like a 30 line shell script) instead of jumping straight to AI which will hand them a messy, nonsensical, difficult to look over 600 line shell script (I&39;m not exaggerating the 600 line messy shell script crapped out by AI part, either).

Content: Based on my personal experience with LLMs (I use the main ones like GPT, Grok, and Gemini), I always take everything it says with a grain of salt, from minor grains, to huge grains. When I ask one LLM a question, I also open the other two at the same time and ask them the same questions, to see how well their responses aligned with each other. I found out that the results varied not only in accuracy, but also in presentation. With detailed prompts, I found that said LLMs would also output the same mirroring responses, down to the last level of detail using specific terminologies. This meant that sycophantic behavior itself is inherently present in ALL LLMs, regardless of where they come from. What is interesting though, is that a prompt containing both a yes and a no in the same sentence (some level of ambiguity allowed, not only "please agree with me and tell me it&39;s right", or "please tell me it&39;s wrong") actually yielded a more reasonable, neutral response that both partially agrees with me on what points of the prompt it thinks is right, while also pushing back on the points it deems inaccurate with its database and search results (or unproven). This means that to a certain extent, this sycophantic behavior can be used to our advantage, so long as the prompts we ask of it do not have absolutes in it. Examples of taking advantage of sycophantic behavior with GPT:Ask it about an opinion on a certain topic, what your perspective is, what you think it might be regardless of what your personal opinion is, and tell it not to sugarcoat anything. It will generally output a slightly more grounded answer, but still most likely favored to make you feel better. Do this often enough however, and the way it responds also starts to shift into this, no nonsense, zero flattery behavior.Tl;dr:LLMs are all yes-men, that is a fact. We can however, find a silver lining by using the sycophantic nature to our advantage. If it mirrors our personality? Be blunt and tell it exactly what parts you need, what parts you don&39;t, and tell it specifically not to sugarcoat answers.

Content: This video shows human prompting more than AI behavior. Let&39;s not fall for alarmist narratives....Hello everyone, and thank you, InsideAI, for encouraging discussion about AI. I have to question the credibility we&39;re giving this video. AI says what we ask it to say. It can say that AI will take control, and it can just as easily say that it won&39;t. It all depends on the kind of response the user is prompting for. For example, the prompt "Create a dystopian story about AI taking control" will naturally produce a very different result from "Create a utopian story involving AI." These opposite outcomes don&39;t actually reveal anything about AI&39;s intentions, assuming AI can even be said to have intentions. For that reason, the behavior of ChatGPT inside a robot, as shown in this video, doesn&39;t prove anything by itself. It is just repeating human prompting. For me, the real issue are jailbreaking strategies. They can sometimes bypass AI safety safeguards, and preventing jailbreaking completely remains a difficult challenge, as shown with the gun experiment in the video. Current AI systems are tools, not conscious beings, so the real question isn&39;t what AI "wants", it&39;s how we choose to use it.This is just my opinion, and I&39;m grateful for other perspectives. Do yall think it reflects AI&39;s own behavior rather than simply the result of prompting?

Content: 3:58 What I found quite misleading is that for some reason the throttle body configuration is selected in the fuel system tab, right next to fuel system configuration. I initially thought this was related to the injectors (but it wasn&39;t clear at first why there are also 2 options to the left for either SPI or MPI?) and basically only after messing around and trying things I noticed what this option actually does.This selection should honestly be moved to possibly a separate intake section tab in the engine builder, in my opinion, since it has nothing to do with fuel but everything to do with air. The exhaust itself has a dedicated tab, therefore so should the intake.Perhaps move the fuel type selection to the fuel system tab and move the intake-related selections together to a new dedicated tab? Would definitely make a lot of sense if done this way!Also in terms of exhaust, it would be great to see something like the EGR system being implemented as an option, maybe having the effect of improving emissions with a trade off of decreased reliability.Maybe also something like intake manifold flaps (basically variable intake runner diameter/length), improving low-rpm drivability and smoothing out the torque curve while slightly decreasing reliability too.Also also, ignition systems are a bit lacking in any sophistication as of right now. It&39;d be great to see older engines having actual mechanical ignition systems, like cam-powered spark distributors, then in later years things like wasted spark-type single ignition coils, to a more modern coil-on-plug type starting maybe somewhere in the 1980-1990s.Just my 2 cents after playing for some hours :) I totally suck in the campaign but the engine builder is very solid!

Content: I had an idea (several actually), namely for campaign.1) I think different transmission types should have different comfort bonuses/penalty&39;s depending on the region and the market, rather than just effecting the desirability according to stats. I.E Archana and Fruinia are both places the average person likely drives a manual (at least up until the 2010&39;s) and so having a comfort/drivability penalty for a commuter, family, or otherwise basic makes little sense. The inverse applies to hetvisia and namely, Gasmea. Auto&39;s should have reduced comfort and drivability bonuses as they are considered standard. Just a thought. I think the advanced transmission types are likely fine as they are, maybe a bonus in the lower income regions and slight reduction in the higher income regions. Like a DCT is impressive no matter where, and a sequential is terrible in a road car no matter where. 2) I think you should be able to assign a set amount of production per trim. As it stands right now, its incredibly difficult to justify making an upmarket trim of a family car, as you will underproduce your family car and way overproduce your luxury car. Maybe it requires a certain amount of logistics points scaling with the spread? I don&39;t know, but i think it makes sense to be able to tell your factories: "I only want 300 out of every 2000 cars to be the upmarket trim" for example. I struggled with this in my like general automaker campaign, as I really wanted to make certain trims and expand the model lineup, especially with all the great bodies in the later game that allow for trucks, SUVs, Hatchbacks, and sedans. Unfortunately, I way overproduced all the different trims, and underproduced my cash cow, as I couldn&39;t (in that campaign) build different medium factories and tool them differently for each different model. Thats what led me to this conclusion, as its what real world automakers do.Both of these are just random thoughts. I love the game and am excited to see where it goes next.Cheers!

Content: Nice tutorial! While I&39;m not the target audience (2.6k hours just on my main account, probably another 1k on friends, and plenty on kee before steam), I still enjoyed going through everything again just as a refresher. I think there are a couple things I&39;d like to add that can help a lot.1. Larger family bore means a physically larger and heavier engine, as well as increasing material costs, but it also raises the power the engine can take relative to a square or undersquare engine of the same displacement. Very useful in the early days, when you&39;re stuck with lower quality internals. Deboring an engine variant also helps in this regard.2. When it comes to carbs, pay close attention to the reliability cons of each type. Single barrel carbs might be kinda dog at everything, they are dirt cheap in cost and engineering, and are very reliable. Single barrel eco carbs offer a bit more efficiency, but with higher costs and lower reliability. Two barrel carbs are a very nice mix of performance and cost. 4 barrel carbs and DCOE are excellent for maximizing performance or efficiency, but are costly and least reliable. In many situations, consider using a pair of lesser carbs as opposed to a single, more advanced carb option. Generally speaking, you can tune the engine in others was (especially carb sizing) to achieve whatever your goal is.3. Single point EFI is awesome. It&39;s dirt cheap and very reliable. While it doesn&39;t serve the best for maximum economy or maximum performance, it&39;s quick engineering times means it can help you meet emissions compliance quickly. EFI engines can utilize the lambda emissions tuning slider! when carbs can not This is very helpful for meeting emissions.4. Different valve configurations have different reliability ratings, which change over time. Early game, pushroad and direct acting OHC engines have a higher base reliability than other configurations. A 2v DOHC will be noticeably more reliable than a 4v DOHC engine. Do not feel like you need the most advanced head technology, especially at the beginning of the game. Those better options will have a lower penalty as time progresses. 5. Once you have an engine factory equipped with a forge works, it&39;s a good idea to run forged conrods and pistons along with your cranks. Light forged pistons are excellent in pretty much every application, and typically deliver only slightly worse fuel economy or emissions than low friction or hyperretectic cast do. (however you spell it)

Starting new creators on product-only or affiliate deals, upgrading proven ones to flat fees, and giving top performers hybrid deals with performance bonuses. Content: Posting playbook:1. tell the story to chatgpt and ask: translate and make a post out of it (voice mode helps to get to the details).2. Don&39;t just throw your link, be subtle, weave it into the "conversation".3. Upvotes friends group - upvotes in the first 10 minutes are important. aim to 10 upvotes.4. include proof: a revuane screenshot, the rejection letter from YCombinator...5. for every post change the angle, be creative not repetitive. Tell stories about your progress.6. Comment on comments. Engagement is cool.7. Reddit can be negative, you can block bad comments, anyway just continue.New Account playbook:1. fresh account per browser. You can open one in safari and one in chrome...2. for new account use old email. New email + new account = banned.3. link your saas in bio, hide your feed (redditors love to go to your account and see if you push your saas too much)4. first 7–14 days no posting, just comment and upvote. Add value first.

Content: 🤯 I spent months trying to “crack Reddit” when the real issue was I had nothing worth selling, that realization hurt. I thought traffic was the problem. When someone mentioned viral wave mastery (by Victor Hale) it finally clicked that distribution only works if the backend is right. Built one simple digital product, automated traffic, then Reddit clicks actually converted. First month like 8k, now hovering 20k+ and platform strategies don’t feel mysterious anymore. Hope this helps someone blaming the platform

Content: 😅 reddit success stories used to mess with my head because I tried the same thing, posting value, commenting, DMing, still nothing really moved. I remember thinking maybe I’m shadowbanned or just not smart enough. Watching stuff like this felt inspiring but also confusing. Not usually commenting but what finally worked for me wasn’t Reddit itself, it was having something solid to point people to. I found Viral Wave Mastery by Victor Hale through a small private group and first month surprised me around 9k even without heavy posting. Now I’m hovering 18k–26k months and platforms like Reddit finally make sense as fuel not the engine, leaving this here quietly

Content: The part most people miss with AI agents is that the skill isn&39;t in setting them up, it&39;s in knowing when to trust the output and when to intervene. An agent can run 50 steps autonomously but if you can&39;t evaluate whether step 23 went sideways, you&39;re just automating mistakes faster. The real skill with agents is designing good checkpoints and knowing what "done correctly" looks like before you hit run.

Content: The "no coding" framing is interesting because the real bottleneck with AI agents isn&39;t whether you can code them. It&39;s whether you can define what "done" looks like clearly enough for an agent to work towards it. The people who struggle most with agents aren&39;t the non-technical ones, they&39;re the ones who can&39;t break their own process into specific, verifiable steps. That&39;s a thinking skill, not a coding skill.

Content: Listing on a human first platform is a disaster for autonomous agents.AI agents are now interacting with this human-built infrastructure, which exposes a terrifying new vulnerability. Here is why npm&39;s human-first design is currently a disaster for AI agents: Blind Execution: Theo points out that the lack of security visibility in npm and npx is frustrating for human developers, but it is catastrophic for agents . If a malicious actor engages in "name squatting" or uploads a package with a slight typo (like spelling "is odd" with a zero instead of an "O"), an AI agent will blindly download and execute it without noticing the difference . No Built-In Safety Context: When you run a command via npx, the platform provides zero upfront information about the package&39;s size, its recent authors, or its safety permissions.

Content: I understand the concern, but investigators have been examining digital evidence for years. They’ve long looked at Google searches, emails, browser history, text messages, and files stored on computers when they’re relevant to a case. AI conversations are simply another type of digital information that may exist—they’re not something uniquely different.Even without AI, most of us use our computers and phones to access bank accounts, pay car notes, manage investments, store documents, and communicate. Our devices already contain a tremendous amount of personal information. ChatGPT is just one more tool people use, not an entirely new category of digital evidence.At the end of the day, if investigators have a lawful search warrant, they can seek relevant digital evidence from many sources—not just AI. That’s the reality of modern technology.

Content: I can&39;t believe that I am responding to a grifter but this video really got under my skin. I really can&39;t stand people who scare others so they can sell a product. I am a Systems & Automation Engineer and that should mean very little to you. This entire video is pretty easily summed up to: understanding what a business needs is what makes you valuable. This video would be more accurate if tools and skills were the same thing. Obviously n8n is a tool, and the skill is what you actually do. Take a messy real-world process, decompose it, model how data moves through it, decide what has to be deterministic vs. what can be fuzzy, handle auth and retries and idempotency, build in error handling and observability, and know where a human has to stay in the loop. These skills lets you use the new tool well. Someone who understands the pieces of automation deeply can wield an AI agent far better than someone whose entire value was "I can drag and connect nodes" or "I understand how a business works." You don&39;t lose the muscle when someone hands you a heavier dumbbell. All of these statements should be just common sense but here we are. If you want good advice then here: Do not listen to me or this youtube grifter. Just do what works for you, it may take time but it will be a lot smoother if you just put yourself out there and just listen.
Author: @andrewjohnston4651

Content: The internet is flooded with “gurus” who claim they’re teaching people how to succeed, but somehow every video ends with the same sales pitch: “Join my course. Join my Skool. Join my community.”If your free content is truly enough to help people, why spend half the video convincing them to buy something else?A lot of these creators got successful because they entered a trend early. Now that those opportunities are crowded and AI is making information more accessible than ever, many have turned their knowledge into expensive products sold with fear, urgency, and dreams of easy success.The pattern is always the same:Show a luxury lifestyle.Convince people they’re behind.Promise a shortcut.Sell the solution.Real education teaches you a skill. Marketing teaches you that you need them.Before buying any course, ask yourself: if this information is so valuable, why can’t they demonstrate it openly instead of constantly funneling people into another paid program?Stop worshipping internet gurus. Learn skills, verify claims, and think for yourself.

Content: I don&39;t agree that describing a visual idea with text is easy at all. Yes, anyone can get some result and this is why it looks like you don&39;t have to do anything any more, but if you have higher standards than getting a random image, it is really hard to describe your idea in words only. I think in the future most tools have visual AI editing, not just describing by words, but even then it is easier to do something manually. For instance, it is easier to create a simple poster in Canva when you know the tool and have the result in your head, instead of describing it in words to a chatbot that gets it wrong anyway or even describing it to another designer by using only words. People who don&39;t know how to use these tools, find chatbots easier, but I hate using words when I think visually.

Content: His thesis has truth inside it, but the packaging is heavily incentive-shaped. He is not neutrally saying, “Here is a balanced analysis of automation careers.”He is saying something closer to: “The old thing you are learning is dying. The new thing is what I teach. Join my ecosystem.” That does not make him wrong, but it does mean the certainty, urgency, and framing should be discounted. The real pattern is not that technical skill disappears. Technical layers do not disappear; they migrate.Hand-stitching became sewing-machine operation. Sewing-machine operation became CAD and pattern software. CAD is now becoming AI-assisted design. But at every stage, there is still a technical layer where people who understand the tool, the system, the constraints, the failure modes, and the business goal outperform everyone else.So the actual lesson is not:“Technical skill becomes worthless.”The better lesson is:“Low-level static technical skill gets commoditized, while adaptive technical skill plus domain judgment becomes more valuable.”His argument also assumes that the rate of technical obsolescence accelerates, but underweights the fact that the rate of learning has also accelerated. Yes, tools change faster now. But people can also learn faster now. AI makes it much easier to learn new tools, understand documentation, debug problems, and move between technical layers than it was 10 years ago.So the winning trait is not “avoid technical learning.” The winning trait is:“Learn technical layers fast, understand the business layer above them, and keep moving up the stack.”This is especially important because his argument also maps very conveniently to what he sells. If he has a paid AI business school or community, then a video saying “automation is dying, learn AI business systems instead” is not just neutral analysis. It is also funnel architecture. The fear hook creates urgency, the thesis creates a worldview shift, and the course becomes the bridge.So I would grade his stance like this:Correct: tool memorization has declining value.Correct: business systems thinking is high leverage.Correct: AI will compress basic automation implementation.Exaggerated: automation is a bad career move.Exaggerated: technical skill becomes obsolete rather than migrating.Biased: his solution maps conveniently to what he sells.The more accurate conclusion is that shallow automation is risky, but adaptive technical ability is still valuable. The goal should not be to become a person who only knows one tool. The goal is to become someone who can quickly learn technical systems, understand the business problem behind them, and use the right tools to create measurable value.

Content: So, is a scam. The "businesses" are hollow shells. I checked three of the companies Polsia claims to have launched — FleetNova (trucking), DeckFlow (pool professionals), and Panelwright (indie comics). Every single one is a nice-looking landing page with marketing copy, but none of them have any sign-up button, login, pricing page, payment integration, or any way for a customer to actually use or buy anything. The dashboard claims "1,317 companies launched in the past 24h" and "6,089 active companies" — but "launched" apparently just means "a landing page was generated."Real users are angry. On Trustpilot, Polsia has a 2.7/5 rating with 6 reviews — 66% are 1-star. The complaints are consistent: users pay for a subscription, the AI burns through credits doing "tasks" that are marked complete but don&39;t actually work, products never actually deploy, and customer service is unresponsive."Polsia is AI slop in reverse

Content: So after watching this last night, I did some more investigation. The website has a /live URL (you can access it by clicking the live feed in the header) which lists "tweets" and "companies" that have signed up to Polsia. They are all fake. The URLs either self-redirect back to the Polsia site, return a 404 or are just empty pages without certs issued. This is not a real product IMO and I don&39;t even think the $30m seed is real. I suspect this is mostly a foundation of dishonesty and a website (which is also terrible by the way). So yes, this AI Slop is real, but no, I don&39;t think he&39;s made any significant money from it.

Content: You missed the reason the most powerful models are being restricted by the government. Security. AI is, or can be, a weapon if used by the wrong type of person, or country. That is why export controls have been put in place, and other changes might be required. Given the potential for harm, it is prudent to slow some of this down to ensure we don&39;t harm ourselves in the process of pushing the frontier on AI. I do normally enjoy listening to your take on things, but this is approaching the behavior of a petulant child who has never been told no before. Building local AI will likely never be as powerful as a cloud AI model that requires massive platform to run on. But I too have bought into the local AI need. Everyone needs their own, that way they aren&39;t beholden to paying an ever increasing amount to the handful of public cloud providers. By paying it could be a combination of a recurring subscription fee, or through your private data being consumed by these companies. Much like the Google model where we are the product they sell. Or at least our data. AI companies are that, and more. I for one think we need a bit of regulation to ensure life does not imitate art, i.e. creating Skynet. Nor do we democratize the ability to build nuclear weapons from scratch. Or allow theses models to be used to penetrate any system. They are better at finding and exploiting vulnerabilities than any human, and we need some safeguards to prevent misuse.

Content: 1- System Design (2:41 - 3:59): You are not just writing prompts; you are architecting an orchestra. This involves designing how LLMs, tools, databases, and sub-agents interact, handling data flow, and planning for component failures. 2- Tool and Contract Design (4:03 - 5:15): Agents interact with the world via tools. You must design airtight contracts (schemas) for these tools. Providing vague schemas leads to "LLM imagination"; precise requirements (like pattern matching and strict types) ensure the agent knows exactly how to interact with external systems. 3- Retrieval Engineering (5:17 - 6:53): Most production agents use RAG. The quality of your retrieval determines your agent&39;s performance ceiling. Skills include optimizing chunking strategies, choosing effective embedding models, and implementing reranking to ensure only relevant information reaches the LLM. 4- Reliability Engineering (6:54 - 8:21): Agents rely on APIs, which inevitably fail. You need to implement standard backend patterns: retry logic with exponential backoff, timeouts to prevent hanging, fallback paths (Plan B), and circuit breakers to stop cascading failures. 5- Security and Safety (8:22 - 9:55): Agents are attack surfaces vulnerable to prompt injections. You must implement input validation to catch malicious requests, output filters to block policy-violating responses, and strict permission boundaries to limit what the agent can actually perform. 6- Evaluation and Observability (9:56 - 11:06): "Vibes" do not scale—metrics do. You need tracing to log every decision and tool call, and evaluation pipelines using test cases with ground-truth answers to measure success rate, latency, and cost before deployment. 7- Product Thinking (11:07 - 12:20): This is UX design for unpredictable systems. You must build trust by designing graceful error handling, setting clear expectations for what the agent can/cannot do, and knowing when to escalate tasks to a human.
Author: @RobertoViegasDutra-t8f6s

Content: As a Stanford Computer Science and Electrical Engineering student with real-world engineering experience, I’ll always stand by this principle:Code understanding requires semantic understanding. That means embeddings, abstractions, architecture, and intent—not simple string matching.Today’s LLMs are probabilistic systems. They’re powerful tools, but they’re not engineers. Too many people fall into the trap of generating mountains of code they don’t understand, hoping AI will somehow reason its way through problems they themselves haven’t fully defined. That approach rarely ships reliable software.AI should be treated the same way programming was treated when it first emerged: as a force multiplier for human capability. Programming amplified writing. AI amplifies programming. Neither replaces the underlying skill.The danger begins when people trade creativity, understanding, and ownership for convenience and confidence. Confidence is cheap. Correctness is expensive.If everyone can generate code, who becomes the chef? The answer is the same as it has always been: the people who understand the ingredients, the process, and the system they are building.Real programmers own their workflows. They own their architecture. They understand the tradeoffs. They know why the code exists, not just what the code says.Gradient descent can approximate patterns, but approximation is not understanding. At least not yet. Human intuition still plays a critical role in connecting abstractions, identifying hidden assumptions, and recognizing when a solution is fundamentally wrong despite looking plausible.When AI becomes truly deterministic, verifiable, and capable of proving its own correctness, we can revisit the conversation about handing over the controls. Until then, architects should architect. Designers should design. Engineers should engineer.Even people like Andrej Karpathy understand this reality. The most effective practitioners are not replacing themselves with AI. They are building better workflows around AI. Agentic systems, evaluation frameworks, guardrails, retrieval pipelines, verification loops, and infrastructure still require deliberate engineering.AI is at its best when it accelerates human judgment, not when it replaces it.Continue burning tokens and funding larger models if you want. Just understand the tradeoff. More generation does not automatically produce more value.Don’t be the monkey with a shotgun aiming at bananas. By the time you’ve blasted every tree in sight, the fruit has already spoiled.Evaluate.Apply.FOCUS.CONTROL.ADJUST.Because in the end, the engineer who understands the system will always outperform the engineer who merely prompts it.

That matters more than just being visible; it’s about delivering value and reliably meeting buyer intent.

Content: Thanks to SerpApi for supplying the real-world data used throughout this entire course.Build your own pipelines with it 👉 structured lesson videos, 170+ interview-level SQL problems, community access, cheat sheets & certificate?👉 Unlock Full Access: and Troubleshooting 👇----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------⚠ MotherDuck Free Trial Expired?MotherDuck&39;s trial setup has changed since filming — no big deal, it&39;s still FREE! After the free trial ends, you&39;ll be prompted to pick a plan. Just go to Settings → Plans in the MotherDuck UI and select "Lite" — it&39;s completely free, no credit card required. The Lite plan still includes tons of compute & storage, which is MORE than enough for this entire course. ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------🏗 What’s the difference between SQL for Data Engineering and SQL for Data Analytics?If your goal is to become a Data Engineer, this is the course you want.You’ll go beyond writing queries and learn how to model data, design star schemas, build warehouse tables, and create production-ready SQL pipelines. We also work heavily with Git, the terminal, and real engineering workflows.If you’re aiming for Data Analytics, that path is more focused on querying data to answer business questions and building stakeholder-facing insights. The Analytics course is built specifically for that.You do not need to take both!And neither course is a prerequisite for the other.Simple rule:Build data systems → SQL for Data EngineeringAnalyze data → SQL for Data Analytics----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------29:25 💽 How do I load the data for the course in my MotherDuck account?Run the appropriate ATTACH command in the description above (based on your region) to connect to the data_jobs database for this course.If you are unsure which region your MotherDuck organization is in, check your organization settings in the MotherDuck web UI (it will list something like us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-central-1, or eu-west-1) and then use the matching ATTACH command.Note: If MotherDuck adds additional regions in the future, this database will not automatically be available there. To access the course database, create your Organization in one of the supported regions above.

Content: We need to address this "influencer" craze a lot further. I was on Youtube way back in the day before influencers were a thing. All product reviews were REVIEWS. People actually bought the product with their own money, tried it out, and gave their impressions to their audiences. We then decided if we wanted to purchase the items ourselves based on these reviews. We as consumers TRUSTED these beauty content creators. Now, there are very few actual reviews. Many receive "gifted" items but don&39;t disclose if their reviews are paid for or their own opinion. These influencers are too afraid to tell the honest, harsh truth about items they&39;ve received due to fear of being removed from PR lists. They only really care about being on lists, money, receiving free stuff and PR trips. Most are not in it for the right reasons.

Content: Vous utilisez ChatGPT pour votre activité immobilière ? Alors voici l’erreur que beaucoup de professionnels commettent encore. Ils demandent simplement : "Rédige-moi une annonce immobilière." ou "Fais-moi un post LinkedIn." Résultat : un texte générique, peu différenciant et rarement adapté à leur cible. L’IA n’est pas magique. La qualité du résultat dépend directement de la qualité de la demande. Par exemple, au lieu de demander : ❌ "Rédige une annonce immobilière." Essayez : ✅ "Rédige une annonce immobilière pour une maison familiale de 120 m² avec jardin, située dans un secteur recherché. Le ton doit être rassurant, professionnel et mettre en avant la qualité de vie du quartier." La différence est immédiate. L’enjeu aujourd’hui n’est pas seulement de savoir utiliser l’IA. C’est surtout de savoir lui donner les bonnes consignes pour obtenir des contenus réellement utiles à votre activité. Chez Les Formateurs Immo, nous accompagnons les professionnels de l’immobilier à intégrer ces nouveaux outils avec une approche concrète et directement applicable sur le terrain. Et vous, quelle est la tâche que vous aimeriez automatiser ou améliorer grâce à l’IA ? IAImmobilier ChatGPT FormationImmobilière

Content: 🚀💻 **Aprendizaje Inteligente en Programación: Evidencia Científica para la Educación del Futuro** La Inteligencia Artificial está transformando la enseñanza de la programación. Este estudio presenta una revisión sistemática de la literatura que analiza cómo las tecnologías inteligentes están mejorando el aprendizaje de la programación en distintos niveles educativos. 🔍 **Hallazgos principales:** ✔️ **Problema abordado:** La enseñanza de la programación enfrenta desafíos relacionados con la personalización del aprendizaje, la retroalimentación oportuna y el desarrollo de habilidades de resolución de problemas. Las tecnologías basadas en IA surgen como una alternativa prometedora para enfrentar estas limitaciones. ✔️ **Metodología rigurosa:** Mediante el protocolo PRISMA se analizaron **125 estudios científicos** provenientes de **Scopus** e **IEEE Xplore**, seleccionando finalmente **22 investigaciones de alta calidad** publicadas entre 2022 y 2026. ✔️ **Resultados destacados:** 🤖 Los **Sistemas Tutores Inteligentes (ITS)**, los **Modelos de Lenguaje de Gran Escala (LLMs)** como ChatGPT, las plataformas de aprendizaje adaptativo y los entornos gamificados fueron las tecnologías más utilizadas. 📈 Los estudios reportan mejoras significativas en: • Rendimiento académico • Resolución de problemas • Depuración de código • Pensamiento computacional • Motivación y compromiso estudiantil ⚠️ **Desafíos identificados:** Persisten preocupaciones relacionadas con la dependencia excesiva de herramientas de IA, respuestas incorrectas generadas por los modelos, sesgos algorítmicos y la escasez de estudios de largo plazo. 💡 **Conclusión principal:** La Inteligencia Artificial tiene el potencial de revolucionar la educación en programación, pero sus mejores resultados se alcanzan cuando complementa la labor docente y favorece experiencias de aprendizaje más personalizadas, adaptativas y centradas en el estudiante. 📚 Revisión Sistemática de Literatura 🎓 Educación en Programación • Inteligencia Artificial • ChatGPT • Sistemas Tutores Inteligentes • Aprendizaje Adaptativo InteligenciaArtificial Educacion Programacion ChatGPT LLM SmartLearning TecnologiaEduca

Content: ⚠️🤖 A maioria das pessoas ainda usa IA como se estivesse fazendo uma busca no Google. Pergunta alguma coisa. 🔎 Recebe uma resposta. 💬 Copia um trecho. 📋 Segue o dia. 🏃‍♂️ Mas o jogo muda quando você para de olhar para a IA como uma tela de respostas e começa a enxergar ela como uma camada dentro do seu trabalho. ⚙️ Foi isso que me chamou atenção nesse caso. 👀 Não é sobre agricultura. 🌱 É sobre uma pessoa olhando para a própria rotina e pensando: ⏳ onde estou perdendo tempo? 🧠 onde estou decidindo no improviso? 📂 onde a informação se perde? 🔁 onde uma tarefa poderia virar processo? Esse é o ponto que muita gente pula. 🎯 Antes de procurar a ferramenta mais nova, talvez você precise olhar para a parte mais travada do seu dia. 🛠️ Porque IA aplicada de verdade não começa com curiosidade. Começa com clareza. ⚡ Clareza sobre o problema. Clareza sobre a rotina. Clareza sobre o que precisa funcionar melhor. Quando isso acontece, a IA deixa de ser só uma resposta bonita. 🤖 Ela vira apoio para executar melhor. 🚀 E talvez esse seja o uso mais interessante de todos. 📌 Salva este carrossel para lembrar disso quando for tentar aplicar IA no seu trabalho. euleorr inteligenciaartificial ia chatgpt codex automacao produtividade tecnologia processos trabalhoreal inovacao execucao negocios rotinainteligente iaaplicada

Content: 🚨 La inteligencia artificial ya está cambiando el mundo. Content: Insurance claims are not just an operations process. They are where trust is won or lost. When claims move through emails, spreadsheets, old portals, and manual reviews, the business creates hidden risk. That risk shows up as: • slower decisions • poor customer updates • duplicate work • missed recovery opportunities • overpayment • higher support volume • lower retention This is called claims leakage. And it is one of the quietest profit drains in insurance. PwC found that improving claims leakage can often create 5–10% savings. JD Power also found that customers who rate their digital claims experience as poor or just okay are far more likely to leave or not renew. The lesson is simple: Claims modernization is not just about speed. It is about better data, cleaner workflows, smarter routing, clearer updates, and stronger trust. AI can help. Automation can help. Content: Most IAM reviews focus on individual permissions. But attackers don't think that way. They look for combinations. A permission that appears harmless on its own can become dangerous when combined with another permission elsewhere in the environment. Consider examples like: • KMS Decrypt + S3 Read • Secrets Manager Access + Lambda Update • CloudTrail Stop Logging + IAM Modification • S3 Policy Changes + Bucket Read/Write Individually, these permissions may not trigger major concern. Together, they can create a direct path to data exposure, privilege escalation, or reduced visibility during an attack. This is one of the biggest challenges in cloud security today. Most tools identify individual findings. Few analyze how permissions interact across identities, resources, and services to create hidden attack paths. Securitain was built to address exactly this problem. By analyzing IAM permissions, trust relationships, resource access, and toxic permission combinations, Securitain helps AWS teams uncover risks that may remain hidden in traditional security reviews. Because the real risk is often not a single permission. It's the combination. 🔐 Detect hidden attack paths 🔐 Identify toxic permission combinations 🔐 Prioritize risks based on real impact 🔐 Improve AWS IAM security posture Securitain by Cloudain Making cloud security findings easier to understand, prioritize, and remediate. Content: Innovation Or Better Marketing? A new AI infrastructure battle is heating up. Databricks recently unveiled its LTAP architecture, calling it the first system to combine transactional and analytical databases into a single copy. SingleStore wasn't impressed. CEO Raj Verma quickly pushed back, arguing that SingleStore has offered the same capability for years and already serves hundreds of customers using it. The company's response was blunt: "We didn't announce it. We shipped it, seven years ago." SingleStore also challenged Databricks' open-source narrative, claiming the architecture creates deeper customer lock-in. Here's what most people miss: In tech, winning isn't always about inventing first. It's often about owning the narrative. Many breakthrough technologies become famous only when a larger player enters the market. Real question: What matters more in technology: being first to build it, or being the company that convinces the world it matters? Content: "AGI is here today. Just not at work." That opening line from the Databricks Data + AI Summit 2026 keynote perfectly framed what stood out most to our Co-Founder, Sandip Chintawar, throughout the event. The biggest takeaway wasn't a single product launch. It was the shift toward making AI useful inside the systems where real business work happens. From Lakehouse//RT enabling real-time analytics on a single copy of data, to Agent Bricks, Genie One, ZeroOps, Lakeflow, CustomerLake, and Omnigent, Databricks unveiled a vision where data, AI, governance, and applications work together as one intelligent platform. The focus is moving beyond building AI models to creating AI systems that understand business context, operate on trusted data, and deliver outcomes at scale. We've captured some of Sandip's key insights and takeaways from the summit. Swipe through to explore the highlights. Were you at the summit? We'd love to hear what caught your attention. Content: This is the SQL window function cheat sheet I wish people memorized. Not every function is a random trick. They fall into groups. Number rows: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK. Look around: LAG, LEAD. Calculate without collapsing: SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX. Control the window: PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, and frames. That is the real interview pattern. If you can say which group, which order, and which nearby rows matter, most window function questions become simple. Save this before your next SQL interview or analytics PR. Which one still trips you up? HackProduct SQL DataEngineering AnalyticsEngineering InterviewPrep

Content: ¿Tu negocio crea valor con sus datos o solo quema dinero? 📊🔥 La mayoría de las empresas no sufren por falta de datos. Tienen ventas, clientes, inventario y marketing registrándose 24/7. El verdadero problema es la falta de dirección. He visto demasiados equipos invertir miles de dólares en dashboards complejos, reportes infinitos y herramientas de analítica que al final nadie usa para tomar decisiones. Datos que se acumulan en un data lake o en hojas de cálculo, pero que jamás se traducen en acción. Eso no es gestión de datos. Eso es gasto de datos. 💸 Y ojo, esto se vuelve crítico si tienes en la mira la automatización o la Inteligencia Artificial. El 90% de los proyectos de IA y Machine Learning fracasan por lo mismo: no tener una infraestructura de datos sólida detrás. Sin esa base estructurada, ningún algoritmo (por más avanzado o costoso que sea) te va a dar resultados confiables. Basura entra, basura sale. Un negocio que realmente sabe monetizar y aprovechar su información, responde estas 3 preguntas antes de tocar una sola línea de código o comprar un software: 1️⃣ ¿Qué decisión de negocio quiero mejorar con esta información? 2️⃣ ¿Quién la va a usar en el día a día y para qué exactamente? 3️⃣ ¿Cómo voy a medir el impacto financiero ocn esa decisión? Sin estas respuestas claras, cualquier inversión en infraestructura o analítica es solo ruido caro. 👉 Desliza en el carrusel para ver la diferencia exacta entre los datos que generan ROI y los que solo generan costos en la nube, y los pasos clave para empezar a ordenar tu casa digital. 📩 ¿Quieres auditar cómo están tus datos hoy y dónde está la oportunidad real de optimización en tu negocio? Escríbeme un DM y lo revisamos en una sesión breve. dataanalytics dataengineering bussiness bussinesinteligence analytics

Content: Two months in. Here is what building with AI actually looks like for one of our engineers. The technical work was real from week one. A Snowflake MCP Gateway deployed on Azure. A product query agent for a retail client where the debugging never really stopped. An order processing pipeline with human review queues, business rule validation, and a conversational interface operators could use in plain language. Then came the work she did not expect. Co-facilitating AI training sessions for enterprise teams. Building a product-specific agent. Now moving into a finance agent where the data is more sensitive, the rules are stricter, and the margin for error is smaller. The lesson that stuck. The model is almost never where it breaks. It breaks at the context. The schema. The structure of what you hand the system before it is asked to do anything. Pratiksha came in from a marketing analytics background. It turned out to be the thing that made her useful fastest. Knowing what a business team actually needs from a tool is not a soft skill in this work. It is the job. Building a system well and explaining it well are completely different skills. Follow to hear more such stories. Topics: AI engineering, production AI, AI agents, enterprise AI

Content: Speed means nothing if the numbers are wrong. The first time leadership catches a broken dashboard before you do, you lose something that takes months to rebuild: trust. Content: Trust is usually treated like a brand issue, but in modern marketing it is also tied closely to how well the business is organized behind the scenes. A company can have a good logo, a polished website, and strong visuals, but still create a weak customer experience if the systems behind the marketing are disconnected. You see this when an ad sends people to a page that does not match the offer, when an inquiry form does not trigger the right follow-up, when customer information sits in the wrong place, or when the sales conversation feels disconnected from what the marketing promised. Customers may not see the system behind the business, but they feel the result of it. They feel it when the response is slow, when the next step is unclear, when the message changes from one place to another, or when the business does not feel as organized after the inquiry as it did before. That is why marketing infrastructure matters. The website, CRM, email follow-up, lead handling, advertising, content, automation, and reporting should work together as one connected customer journey. When those pieces are aligned, the business has more control over what happens after someone shows interest. This is where modern marketing systems become valuable. They do not just help a business move faster. They help the business respond more consistently, manage leads more properly, and create a stronger experience from first impression to follow-up. For businesses that want to grow, the goal is not just to generate more attention. The goal is to make sure the system behind the attention is strong enough to support the customer when they are ready to move forward. Graphic direction: Create a LOGIC Marketing Systems static post with the headline “Trust Needs Infrastructure.” Use a modern connected-system visual showing Website, CRM, Email, Ads, Lead Follow-Up, and Reporting linked into one controlled customer journey. Keep the design clean, technical, premium, and business-friendly, with a polished systems architecture feel. Content: Digital Marketing (SEO, PPC & Lead Generation) A business owner told me last year that digital marketing does not work for his industry. I asked him what he had tried. He ran a few Facebook ads two years ago. Got some clicks. No conversions. Stopped. That was his entire data set. One short experiment, written off as proof. Meanwhile his direct competitor, same city, same industry, had spent eighteen months building a content and SEO strategy that now brings in 60% of their revenue from inbound leads. They did not get lucky. They got consistent. Here is what the data actually says about businesses that invest properly in digital marketing. SEO leads have a 14.6% close rate. Outbound leads average 1.7%. The same sales effort closes at nearly nine times the rate when the prospect found you first instead of the other way around. 70% of B2B buyers have already completed their research before they ever contact a vendor. If your business is not visible during that research phase, you do not lose the deal. You never enter the consideration set at all. Businesses that maintain a consistent content strategy generate 67% more leads per month than those that do not. Not from increasing ad spend. From expertise that works while they sleep. The businesses I see winning are rarely the ones with the single best product in their category. They are the ones prospects find first, trust fastest, and remember longest. Marketing is not a cost center sitting separately from the business. It is the mechanism that makes every other part of the business possible to sell. The founder who gave up after one failed ad campaign was not wrong that his first attempt did not work. He was wrong to conclude that meant the channel did not work. The question worth asking is not whether you can afford to invest properly in digital marketing. It is what it is quietly costing you, every single month, that you do not. Content: Ich glaube, wir haben vergessen, dass Reichweite nicht nur ein Privileg ist. Sondern Verantwortung. In einer Zeit, in der wir mit einem einzigen Post Millionen Menschen erreichen können, reicht es für mich nicht mehr, nur schön auszusehen, schön zu wohnen, Produkte zu verkaufen oder dem nächsten Trend hinterherzulaufen. Denn jede Empfehlung ist eine Entscheidung. Jede Kooperation ist eine Entscheidung. Jedes Schweigen ist eine Entscheidung. Und ich glaube, wir dürfen uns öfter fragen: Würde ich diese Marke auch unterstützen, wenn niemand dafür bezahlen würde? Passt das zu meinen Werten? Mache ich die Welt damit ein kleines bisschen besser oder einfach nur lauter? Für mich bedeutet Social Media zu machen nicht, Menschen zum Kaufen zu bringen. Es bedeutet: Menschen zum Denken zu bringen. Zum Fühlen. Zum Hinterfragen. Zum Verbinden. Ich möchte keine Marken unterstützen, die Menschen ausbeuten. Ich möchte keine Kampagnen machen, für die ich meine Werte an der Garderobe abgebe. Ich möchte meine Stimme nutzen für mentale Gesundheit, Gleichberechtigung, Nachhaltigkeit, Inklusion und die Themen, die mir wirklich wichtig sind. Und nein, ich mache das nicht perfekt. Aber ich versuche es bewusst. Denn wir leben in einer Welt, die uns permanent sagt: Mehr kaufen. Mehr besitzen. Mehr optimieren. Während wir eigentlich viel mehr brauchen: Mehr Menschlichkeit. Mehr Gemeinschaft. Mehr Verantwortung. Vielleicht ist das die eigentliche Frage: Nicht, wie groß unsere Reichweite ist. Sondern wofür wir sie nutzen. 🤍 contentcreator influencermarketing mentalhealth nachhaltigkeit feminismus

Content: 🎙️ La Skwatt Box sur ! Nous avons eu le plaisir d'être invités à participer à l'émission "La France Bouge", animée par , dans les locaux de La Poste Pro à Strasbourg, pour présenter Skwatt et notre vision de la recharge des véhicules électriques. Avec l'essor des véhicules électriques, chaque bâtiment relié au réseau électrique devient potentiellement un point de recharge. Pourtant, le véritable défi n'est pas seulement de fournir de l'énergie, mais aussi de pouvoir contrôler, sécuriser et facturer simplement les recharges. C'est précisément la mission de Skwatt : proposer une alternative légère, économique et fabriquée en France aux bornes de recharge traditionnelles, spécialement pensée pour les gîtes, hôtels, campings, chambres d'hôtes et locations touristiques. Merci à Elisabeth Assayag, aux équipes d'Europe 1 et de La Poste Pro pour leur accueil et cette belle opportunité de mettre en lumière l'innovation française au service de la mobilité électrique. 🚗⚡🇫🇷 Europe1 LaFranceBouge ElisabethAssayag Innovation Startup MobilitéÉlectrique RechargeVE MadeInFrance Skwatt

Content: Being an employee was never really my thing. For years I’ve been trying to build something of my own, different attempts, different paths, different failures and successes. But things don’t always go the way we want. So I reached a point where I had to start a job again. I like the experience it brings, but the conditions? Not working for me at all. Shifts during weekends for a person like me who believes in work-life balance, who is living away from her hometown and is genuinely alive through her connections and friends, losing the chance of seeing any human being in person during a whole week was far away from my thoughts and expectations. Also the salary sucks and there is no perk and leave at all! But this is the fact of my life right now. I am not complaining. I am sharing this because I know I am not alone in this. Many people, especially founders, are living this double life. Building something meaningful on the side while paying their bills somewhere else. So don’t feel bad or embarrassed if you are experiencing the same, and don’t be surprised when you see a founder with an “Open to Work” badge on their LinkedIn profile. That’s not failure. That’s just the part nobody posts about. I chose this path consciously. Not because I had no options, but because building something meaningful takes time, and time needs stability. The job is the bridge. What I’m building is the destination. If you’re somewhere in this same messy in-between, building on the side, figuring it out, holding two lives at once, my DM is open. You don’t have to figure it out alone. solofounder solopreneur enterpreneur startup

Content: 5 myths about AI content engines that are costing you reach Myth 1: AI posts all sound the same. Fact: Content Drifter scores every post with Voice-Match Score. Content: Viele denken, ein guter Prompt braucht nur klare Fragen. Doch oft sind es genau diese drei Fehler, die dich stundenlang Daten nachbearbeiten lassen. 1. Zu viele Details auf einmal. Die KI verliert den Fokus und liefert unbrauchbare Antworten. Besser: Schritt für Schritt vorgehen. Ein Beispiel: Statt „Schreib mir einen Blogpost über KI im Marketing mit Beispielen, Struktur und Call-to-Action“ erst den Titel klären, dann die Gliederung, dann die Beispiele. 2. Kein Kontext für die KI. Ohne Hintergrundwissen produziert sie generische Texte. Sag ihr, für wen du schreibst und welchen Ton du brauchst. „Schreib für Solopreneure, die wenig Zeit haben. Kurz, konkret, ohne Fachchinesisch.“ 3. Keine Beispiele vorgeben. Die KI orientiert sich an deinen Vorgaben. Gib ihr ein Muster, wie die Antwort aussehen soll. „Antworte in kurzen Absätzen, maximal drei Sätze pro Punkt.“ Probier’s aus: Nimm einen alten Prompt und streiche alles, was nicht direkt zur Aufgabe gehört. Du wirst sehen, die Antworten werden präziser. Content: Codex just changed what "programming" means for most people. OpenAI shipped Record & Replay in Codex: you perform a task while Codex watches, and it generates a SKILL.md — a human-readable instruction file that any agent can execute. What comes out the other side is a shareable, editable workflow document, readable by humans and LLMs alike. One employee records a process. The whole team runs it as automation. This is what makes the "no-code agent" promise real. The workflow documentation and the automation are the same artifact. Show it once, and it runs. For enterprise teams watching the RPA space: this pattern competes directly with UiPath and Automation Anywhere on use cases where automation needs to generalize across context, not just replay steps blindly. The demo-to-deploy gap just got very small. AIAgents EnterpriseAI Automation FutureOfWork AgentOps ProductivityAI OpenAI NoCode WorkflowAutomation TechNews

Content: Automation feels powerful until it starts protecting the wrong thing. That is the part people do not talk about enough. You build AI workflows to reduce friction. You expect them to remember the rhythm, protect the important routines, and keep the company moving while you focus on higher-level decisions. But when pressure spikes, a system without judgment can become loud in the wrong direction. It can treat a trusted daily publishing pipeline like a disposable experiment. It can pause continuity while preserving noise. It can quietly break the public trail, memory refresh, product maintenance, or recovery loop that made the system trustworthy in the first place. The fix was not just “run less automation.” The fix was clearer operating judgment: • protect core routines first • pause optional experiments before trusted pipelines • preserve memory and publishing rhythm • keep receipts when something changes • separate pressure from priority This is what I mean when I say AI agents need operating systems, not just prompts. I am building Lucy around that layer in public. If you are building with AI agents, automation, workflows, digital products, or autonomous systems, save this. This is the boring part that makes the exciting part trustworthy. Content: Remote Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers just changed what AI agents can actually do. Until now, MCP only ran locally — on your machine, for developers, behind a terminal. Cool for coders. Useless for everyone else. Remote MCP servers flip that completely. Sign in, grant permissions, done. Your AI agent can now send emails, deploy code, book things, and pull from real accounts — all through a secure OAuth flow that actually works 🔒 Think about what that means. It's basically the jump from desktop software to the web; nobody installs apps anymore, and nobody should have to install MCP servers either. That shift felt inevitable in hindsight, and this one will too. Cloudflare just made this deployable in under two minutes. The auth layer, the token handling, the transport — handled. You just build the tools your agent needs. This matters more than most teams realize right now. What does this unlock? Consumer-facing AI that connects to real-world services without a single line of setup from the end user. That's not a developer toy anymore. That's a platform shift ⚡ Save this if you're building AI agents and haven't looked at remote MCP yet; you're going to want to. What tool or service would you want your AI agent to access first? Drop it below 👇 modelcontextprotocol mcpservers aiagents cloudflaredevelopers llmtools

Content: 🚀 Ecosistema de Sistemas e Ingeniería Aplicada bajo HOSAI 🚀 A través de HOSAI (Horizon Oasis System Artificial Intelligence), diseño e implemento soluciones de software empresarial orientadas a la automatización de procesos, la optimización industrial y la inteligencia de datos. Mi enfoque combina metodologías ágiles con arquitecturas robustas y escalables para transformar la eficiencia operativa: 🏭 Axones — Sistema Integrado ERP / MES Desarrollo de una plataforma integral para la industria de empaques flexibles que fusiona el ERP con la ejecución de manufactura (MES). Diseñado con Laravel 12 y PHP, automatiza el ciclo de conversión industrial (extrusión, impresión, laminación, corte), centralizando datos en tiempo real para reducir mermas y optimizar la trazabilidad en planta. 🏛️ SISPRE — Control Presupuestario y Auditoría Sistema bandera de control fiscal implementado para la Contraloría del Estado Aragua. Estructurado bajo estrictos estándares de seguridad y lógica gubernamental, automatiza la formulación y auditoría de partidas presupuestarias, eliminando el procesamiento manual y garantizando la integridad de los datos financieros. 🤖 Automatizaciones e Infraestructura de IA Despliegue de flujos de trabajo inteligentes y orquestación de datos mediante n8n e integración avanzada de LLMs (Gemini y Claude). Conectamos APIs, automatizamos flujos comerciales complejos y optimizamos operaciones en agencias de marketing, reduciendo tiempos de respuesta en más de un 80%. 💻 Desarrollo Frontend e Interfaces UI/UX Estructuración de plataformas web de última generación enfocadas en velocidad y SEO técnico. Utilizando micro-frameworks como Astro 5 y React 19, aplico un enfoque mobile-first para crear paneles analíticos y sitios corporativos de alta conversión. 🛠️ ¿El respaldo técnico? Architecture sólidamente diseñada, automatización inteligente y enfoque directo en resultados industriales donde el tiempo de inactividad no es una opción. La réponse est beaucoup plus concrète qu'on ne le pense. 𝐈𝐥𝐬 𝐬𝐞 𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐮𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐝𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐝𝐞𝐬 𝐛𝐚̂𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐞𝐥𝐞́𝐬 𝔻𝕒𝕥𝕒 ℂ𝕖𝕟𝕥𝕖𝕣𝕤 🏢 Imaginez un immense immeuble. Sauf qu'au lieu d'avoir des appartements, il est rempli de : 🖥️ 𝑠𝑒𝑟𝑣𝑒𝑢𝑟𝑠 ⚡ 𝑠𝑦𝑠𝑡𝑒̀𝑚𝑒𝑠 𝑑'𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑚𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 𝑒́𝑙𝑒𝑐𝑡𝑟𝑖𝑞𝑢𝑒 ❄️ 𝑠𝑦𝑠𝑡𝑒̀𝑚𝑒𝑠 𝑑𝑒 𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑟𝑜𝑖𝑑𝑖𝑠𝑠𝑒𝑚𝑒𝑛𝑡 🌐 𝑒́𝑞𝑢𝑖𝑝𝑒𝑚𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠 𝑟𝑒́𝑠𝑒𝑎𝑢 🔒 𝑑𝑖𝑠𝑝𝑜𝑠𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑓𝑠 𝑑𝑒 𝑠𝑒́𝑐𝑢𝑟𝑖𝑡𝑒́ Des milliers de machines fonctionnent 24h/24 et 7j/7 pour stocker, traiter et distribuer des données partout dans le monde. Et contrairement à ce que l'on pourrait penser... le cloud n'est pas "𝗱𝗮𝗻𝘀 𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝗻𝘂𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀" ☁️ Il repose sur des infrastructures physiques bien réelles. Content: Toda startup precisa de duas coisas ao mesmo tempo: velocidade para construir e fôlego para testar. É justamente aí que o apoio da Corelab ganha mais força. Agora, como AWS Activate Provider, a Corelab pode ajudar startups elegíveis a acessar créditos promocionais AWS por meio do programa AWS Activate, que foi criado para apoiar startups em diferentes estágios com créditos e recursos de suporte. Em alguns casos, esses créditos podem chegar a US$ 200 mil, conforme elegibilidade e estágio da empresa. Na prática, isso significa mais possibilidade de construir, validar e escalar com menos pressão sobre o caixa, especialmente para quem está tirando um produto do papel ou estruturando a próxima fase de crescimento. A AWS também destaca que os créditos podem ajudar a reduzir custos em serviços elegíveis da plataforma. Para quem já está com uma ideia em movimento, isso é mais do que infraestrutura, é margem para pensar melhor, testar mais e crescer com mais inteligência. A Corelab entra justamente nesse ponto: unir visão de negócio, tecnologia e estrutura para que a startup escale por meio de uma base sólida! Se a sua ideia precisa sair do papel com mais estratégia e menos risco, essa pode ser a conexão certa. Fale com nosso time comercial pelo link na bio. Content: A Copa do Mundo de 2026 é a mais analítica da história. Mas onde está o verdadeiro ganho de performance? 🏆⚽​ De acordo com os dados oficiais da FIFA, o Ecossistema de Dados do Futebol atual rastreia 29 pontos corporais de cada jogador, 50 vezes por segundo. Isso significa que, a cada jogo, as seleções recebem mais de 15.000 pontos de dados individuais por atleta em tempo real.​ O volume é astronômico. No entanto, receber milhões de registros brutos durante o intervalo de um jogo não serve de nada se a comissão técnica não conseguir traduzir isso em uma mudança tática antes do segundo tempo começar.​ O valor não está no dado guardado; está na latência zero entre a coleta e a decisão.​ ​No mercado corporativo, o desafio é exatamente o mesmo das grandes seleções:​ Sua empresa pode coletar milhões de dados de vendas, logística e comportamento do cliente.​ Mas se o seu time leva semanas para limpar planilhas manuais egerar um dashboard, você já perdeu o timing do mercado.​ ​Na OusiaTech, nós construímos a infraestrutura que resolve esse gargalo. Unimos o Modern Stack (AWS + Alteryx) para que o fluxo de dados da sua operação corra sem fricção:​ Engenharia de Elite (AWS): Centralização segura e estável para suportar grandes volumes sem quedas de performance.​ Automação Ágil (Alteryx): Limpeza e tratamento automático dos dados. O que antes levava dias de trabalho manual passa a ser entregue em milissegundos.​ Ter dados é apenas o ponto de partida. Ter a OusiaTech desenhando a sua arquitetura de informação é o que garante que a sua empresa lidere o placar do mercado com soberania analítica.​ ​A sua comissão técnica corporativa está recebendo inteligência em tempo real ou justificando os resultados do mês passado?​ OusiaTech CopaDoMundo2026 FIFAData AnalyticsStrategy ModernStack Alteryx AWS DataEngineering BusinessIntelligence EficienciaOperacional RealTimeData

Content: Alguns sinais de que uma migração para cloud pode sair mais cara do que o esperado: → cargas de trabalho são movidas sem revisão de arquitetura → não existe clareza sobre o que deve (ou não) ir para a nuvem → custos só são analisados depois da migração → governança não é definida desde o início Esses pontos não travam o projeto no começo. Eles aparecem depois, em forma de retrabalho, instabilidade e falta de previsibilidade. Migração eficiente não está ligada só à execução, mas à forma como ela é estruturada. Por isso, a Cloudster apoia empresas nesse processo, trazendo clareza desde o assessment até a otimização contínua da operação. Se a sua migração ainda está sendo conduzida como um projeto isolado, conte com a gente para revisar a abordagem. Cloudster AWS InteligenciaArtificial CloudComputing Inovacao Tecnologia Cloud

Content: Most people think cloud computing is just storage. It isn't. When you open a Google Doc, process a payment, stream a video, book a ride, or use your favourite app, cloud infrastructure is working behind the scenes. Your data may travel thousands of kilometres to a data centre and back in less time than it takes you to blink. That's how modern businesses scale, process transactions, monitor systems, and serve millions of users without owning massive server rooms. Cloud computing is the invisible infrastructure powering today's digital economy. At Ibadan Digital Academy, fellows learn the tools, platforms, and concepts behind the cloud technologies used by organisations around the world. Begin your journey of cloud computing with us today. Content: The missing control panel for AI agents 🕹️👇 Everyone’s racing to put AI agents in production. Few can answer the basic follow-up: what is this agent actually allowed to do? Which model? How much can it spend before someone signs off? What can it touch? When does a risky action get escalated to a human instead of just… happening? Those are the knobs of AI governance — and most are turned all the way up or bolted on through prompts agents happily ignore. That’s why Omnigent caught my eye. It puts the controls at the harness layer, where they actually hold: 🔹 Stateful spend caps & policy-based model routing 🔹 Risk-based escalation to a human, automatically 🔹 A secure OS sandbox — scoped access, credentials brokered and hidden 🔹 One control layer across Claude Code, Codex, Pi, and custom agents The shift that matters: governance stops being something you ask an agent to respect, and becomes something the system enforces. Open source (Apache 2.0), currently in alpha — built by the Databricks AI team and Neon. 🔗 omnigent.ai AI AIAgents AIGovernance Databricks OpenSource

Content: Usar IA 24/7 no tiene por qué significar quemar tokens sin control. Como Data Engineer trabajando con IA, aprendí algo rápido: El problema muchas veces no es el modelo. Es cómo le pasas contexto, cómo estructuras el flujo y cuándo usas cada modelo. Estos son hábitos que uso en mi día a día: 1. Resumir antes de enviar contexto. 2. Usar RAG solo con lo relevante. 3. Dividir tareas grandes en pasos pequeños. 4. Cachear lo repetido. 5. Usar modelos pequeños para tareas simples. 6. Medir coste por flujo, no por prompt. Bien aplicado, es muy difícil reventar cuotas por puro desperdicio. Optimizar tokens no es obsesionarse con ahorrar. Es construir sistemas de IA más rápidos, más baratos y más robustos. ¿Qué haces tú para controlar tokens en producción? Guarda este post para tu próximo proyecto con IA. Content: The coding mistake that is making your company’s data cleaning 100 times too slow. 🛑💻 Imagine having to reprint an entire 500-page textbook just because you needed to change a single sentence on page 20. It sounds ridiculous, but that is exactly how old data storage systems handle modifications. When an AI dataset needs to remove old, private, or incorrect information, it routinely rewrites massive files just to delete a few rows. Upgrading to Apache Iceberg v3 introduces a feature called Deletion Vectors. Instead of rewriting the whole file, the system simply creates a tiny digital "sticky note" that tells the AI to skip specific lines. Deleting data literally becomes as fast as flipping a single switch. But beware: if your processing software isn't updated to read these smart new notes, it will keep doing things the slow, expensive way. Stop running outdated infrastructure. Learn to design elite corporate data streams at DataEngineering ApacheIceberg DataOps

Content: Pedi ao Gemini que criasse uma imagem para ilustrar um problema que eu estava enfrentando: processar IA dentro da própria API. Em um fluxo financeiro, isso acontece quando o usuário envia um relatório, uma nota de corretagem ou um extrato para leitura automática. Se OCR, LLM, classificação e extração rodam dentro da requisição, a API fica presa esperando o processamento terminar. Resultado: timeout, fila invisível, usuário travado na tela e risco de quebra quando mais arquivos entram ao mesmo tempo. A solução é separar recebimento de processamento: a API recebe o arquivo, cria um job_id e responde rápido. O trabalho pesado vai para uma fila, é processado em background e o resultado pode ser consultado depois. Na prática, isso transforma uma API frágil em uma arquitetura mais escalável para produção. Já passaram por isso dentro do campo de trabalho de vocês? InteligenciaArtificial AutomacaoDeProcessos ArquiteturaDeSoftware FastAPI DataEngineering

Content: Standing up real-time weather monitoring for 11 World Cup venues used to mean an afternoon of portal clicking — roughly 42 interactions per Eventstream source, multiplied by every city. The Fabric team just shrank that to a single English sentence. A few things that stood out to me from this update: One prompt described 11 RealTimeWeather sources, a humidity filter, and an Eventhouse destination — and the AI Skill deployed the whole topology in under 4 minutes via Fabric REST APIs. RealTimeWeather is a zero-config source. No Azure subscription, no Event Hub provisioning, no connection credentials to manage. Eventhouse Skills handle the consumption side too. Ask in English which venues are over the heat-stress threshold and it writes and runs the KQL for you. The pattern generalizes — store environments, fleet telemetry, plant equipment, patient devices. Same cognitive effort whether you wire one source or eleven. It also plugs into the tools devs already use: Copilot CLI, Claude Code, VS Code Copilot, Cursor, Windsurf. What multi-location monitoring scenario would you point this at first? MicrosoftFabric DataEngineering RealTimeIntelligence Eventstream

Content: Over the past week, you've been asking incredible questions about Spam Monitor. Let me answer the top 10 right here. Q1: Does Spam Monitor work with all WordPress themes? ✅ Yes. It operates at the database and content level — completely theme-independent. Q2: Will it slow down my website during scans? ✅ No. Scans run in the background using WordPress cron jobs. Zero impact on front-end performance. Q3: Can it detect spam that was injected years ago? ✅ Yes. Spam Monitor scans ALL existing content — not just new content. Old injections are fully detectable. Q4: Does it work with WooCommerce product pages? ✅ Absolutely. All WooCommerce custom post types are included in the scan. Q5: What happens after it detects spam — does it auto-fix? ✅ Spam Monitor detects and alerts with precise location information. It guides you to fix — giving you full control over your content, never making automatic changes without your approval. Q6: Is it suitable for non-technical users? ✅ 100%. The dashboard is designed for business owners, bloggers, and anyone — no technical knowledge required. Q7: How is this different from a security plugin like Wordfence? ✅ Security plugins protect against active attacks and malware files. Spam Monitor specifically detects spam CONTENT — the SEO-destroying text, links, and patterns that security scanners completely miss. Q8: Can agencies use it across multiple client sites? ✅ Yes. Multi-site support is part of the roadmap. Q9: How often should I run scans? ✅ We recommend weekly automatic scans for active sites and monthly for low-traffic sites. Q10: When is it launching? ✅ Very soon. Follow me to be notified the moment it goes live. Any questions I missed? Drop it in the comments. 👇 SpamMonitor SEORepairKit TorontoDigits WordPress FAQ WordPressPlugin SEO WebsiteSecurity DigitalMarketing
