Content: Коли рекомендація блогера стає продажем? 🌟 Інфлюенсери впливають на покупки частіше, ніж здається. Але найчастіше вони не створюють потребу, а допомагають зробити вибір. 👀 Найбільший вплив рекомендації мають тоді, коли людина вже замислювалася про покупку. Також важливо, щоб блогер сам давно та регулярно користувався продуктом. Для частини аудиторії додатковим стимулом стають спеціальні пропозиції та промокоди, а для інших – рекомендації в нових для себе категоріях. І це не лише теорія. 📊 28% українців уже купували товари або послуги за рекомендацією блогера. Втім, шлях до покупки не завжди прямий. Після реклами в контенті блогерів хтось просто переглядає інтеграцію, хтось переходить за посиланням із цікавості, а дехто зберігає інформацію, щоб повернутися до неї пізніше. Лише частина аудиторії одразу взаємодіє з брендом. 💡 Інфлюенсер-маркетинг працює не лише через миттєві продажі. Часто рекомендація стає ще одним аргументом, який допомагає споживачу впевнитися у своєму виборі.
Author: kantar_ukraine

Content: Рекомендації блогерів: що формує довіру? 👤🤳🏼 На конференції IAB Connect 2026: «Диджитал і Конструктив» Клієнт-партнерка Kantar Україна Оля Капустян представила результати дослідження «Коли рекомендація стає продажем: як інфлюенсери та стрімери впливають на вибір українців». За даними Kantar, 79% українців сьогодні слідкують за контентом інфлюенсерів. Для багатьох це не лише розвага, а й спосіб дізнатися щось нове та орієнтуватися у новинках брендів. 👀 Та чи довіряють українці рекомендаціям блогерів? Лише 17% українців довіряють рекламі інфлюенсерів, тоді як майже половина займає нейтральну позицію. 🤝🏻 Найбільше на довіру впливають чесність, тривалий контакт з автором та відсутність надмірної реклами. Натомість вона швидко руйнується, коли реклама виглядає нещирою або створює відчуття «занадто ідеальної» картинки. 💡 Для брендів це важливий сигнал: аудиторія очікує від інфлюенсерів не бездоганної реклами, а чесних і релевантних рекомендацій.
Author: kantar_ukraine

Content: One sentence from this week's newsletter has been stuck in my head. The business is simply a tool. Your life is the destination. I think a lot of aspiring entrepreneurs skip over that idea. They spend hours researching business ideas, watching YouTube videos, listening to podcasts, and trying to figure out which opportunity has the most potential. That matters, but what matters more is the kind of life you actually want. A business can be profitable and still be a poor fit. If you value flexibility, certain businesses will naturally fit better than others. If your goal is building a large company, you'll make different decisions than someone who wants to stay small and work directly with clients. If spending time with your family is one of your highest priorities, that should influence the type of business you build. There isn't one version of entrepreneurial success. There are thousands. The important part is choosing yours before you start chasing someone else's. That's the topic I explored in this week's edition of **Foundational Fridays** because I think it's one of the most overlooked parts of starting a business. If you missed it, you can catch up using the link in the bio. And if you're still trying to figure out what kind of business is right for you, I think you'll enjoy Foundational Fridays. Every Friday I share one practical lesson from my business school classroom to help you start smart. entrepreneurship101 entrepreneurshipclass entrepreneur businesseducation startup entrepreneurship entrepreneurjourney entrepreneurshiptip

Content: Microsoft, OpenAI’ya milyarlarca dolar yatırdı. Peki bu para nereye gidiyor ve geri dönüşü nasıl? Çoğu kişi bunu filantropi veya saf teknoloji sevgisi sanır. Yanlış. Bu, dijital dünyanın en büyük "kilitlenme" (vendor lock-in) stratejilerinden biri. 1. Altyapı Kiralama: OpenAI, modellerini eğitmek için devasa GPU gücüne ihtiyaç duyuyor. Microsoft, bu gücü Azure üzerinden veriyor. 2. Veri Akışı: OpenAI’nin her hesabı, Azure hesabıdır. Veri, Microsoft’un gözetiminden geçiyor. 3. Gelir Modeli: OpenAI, Azure kullanımı için kâr payı ödeyor. Yani OpenAI büyüdükçe, Microsoft’un cloud geliri katlanarak artıyor. Özetle: Microsoft, OpenAI’yı satın almıyor. OpenAI’yı Microsoft’un en büyük "ürün satıcısı" haline getiriyor. 💸 Bu stratejiyi başka hangi alanda gördünüz? Content: From Research to Product Building security tools taught me that finding vulnerabilities is only part of the challenge. Content: Happy Father’s Day, Daddy ❤️🥺 There are so many things I wanted to tell you, but life didn’t give me enough time⏳ After you left, I spent years missing not just you, but the feeling of having someone who would always stand by me no matter what❤️‍🩹. Every achievement, every heartbreak, and every new beginning in my life carried a silent wish — “I wish my dad was here to see this💔.” Today, I want you to know that I am trying my best. I am building a career, fighting battles you never got to see, and somehow finding the strength to keep going even on the days I want to give up. There are still moments when I need you. There are still days when I wish I could call you, tell you about my achievements, my dreams, my fears, and hear you say that everything will be okay💞. If you were here today, I hope you would be proud of your little girl. No matter how much I grow up, a part of me will always remain the daughter who looked for comfort, strength, and safety in her dad🥺. And if there is one thing I want you to know, it’s that no matter how many years pass, no one can ever take your place in my heart. I miss you every day, but I love you even more. Happy Father’s Day, Daddy. Content: Agentic AI is about to get a lot harder to turn off. The "loopy" shift in AI isn't just a fun buzzword. It's a real architectural change. Instead of one agent doing a task and stopping, you now have swarms of agents running continuously in the background — looping, delegating, self-correcting. This is where things get genuinely interesting for builders. Right now most people are still thinking about AI as a request-response tool. You prompt it, it answers. That mental model is already outdated. The loop model means you're not building a feature anymore. You're building a system that runs autonomously, makes decisions without you, and keeps going. That's a different responsibility entirely. The real questions developers need to answer now: How do you monitor something that never stops? How do you know when it's doing something wrong? What does "done" even mean when the agent is designed to keep going? These aren't theoretical problems. If you're building anything with Claude Code or multi-agent setups right now, you're already bumping into early versions of this. The teams that figure out observability, error handling, and trust boundaries for continuous agents — those are the teams that will actually ship reliable products. Everyone else will have impressive demos that break in production. I've been thinking a lot about this for my own builds. The jump from "agent that completes a task" to "agent that runs forever" is not a small one. What's your current approach to keeping agents under control when they're running autonomously? Drop it in the comments — genuinely curious what's working. claudecode aiagents buildwithAI llm aitools artificialintelligence

Content: Most people think AI Agents are simply better chatbots. They're not. ChatGPT can answer a question. An AI Agent can complete a task. That's the difference. Imagine giving an AI Agent a goal: "Generate 50 qualified sales leads." Instead of producing a response, it could: ✅ Research target companies ✅ Identify decision-makers ✅ Draft outreach messages ✅ Update your CRM ✅ Track responses ✅ Recommend next actions All with minimal human intervention. This is why many technology leaders believe AI Agents represent the next major shift after Generative AI. Generative AI creates content. AI Agents create outcomes. Think about the possibilities: 📈 Sales Agent Prospects, follows up, updates CRM, prepares proposals. 👥 HR Agent Screens resumes, schedules interviews, answers employee questions. 💰 Finance Agent Monitors expenses, reconciles data, flags anomalies. 🎧 Customer Support Agent Handles routine requests and escalates complex cases. ⚙️ Operations Agent Monitors workflows, identifies bottlenecks, and recommends improvements. The most important question for leaders today isn't: "Will AI Agents replace people?" A better question is: "Which repetitive, low-value tasks are preventing my team from doing higher-value work?" The companies that succeed won't simply deploy AI. They will redesign work around humans and AI working together. The future may not be human versus AI. It may be: Humans with AI Agents versus humans without them. If you could assign ONE task to an AI Agent tomorrow, what would it be? 👇 Share your answer below. Content: A proposed bill reportedly introduced by Senator Bernie Sanders has sparked discussion about how the benefits of artificial intelligence should be shared across society. The proposal centers on the idea that as AI companies generate massive economic value through automation and productivity gains, a portion of that wealth could be returned directly to the public. Supporters argue that AI is built on decades of publicly funded research, infrastructure, and data generated by society, making it reasonable for citizens to share in the financial upside. They see such proposals as a potential way to address growing concerns about wealth concentration and job displacement. Critics, however, question the practicality of government-mandated ownership stakes, warning that such measures could discourage investment, innovation, and competition in a rapidly evolving industry. Others argue that alternative approaches, such as taxation or universal dividend programs, may be more feasible. The broader debate reflects a key question of the AI era: who should benefit from the enormous value created by advanced artificial intelligence, and how should those benefits be distributed? . . . . startup ai automation tech entrepreneurship

Together with GRASS, Kardex has successfully tackled this very challenge in Vorarlberg, Austria. 📦⚙️ The result is a highly automated logistics center with approximately 38,800 pallet storage locations, fully automated truck unloading, intelligent buffer solutions, and ergonomic goods-to-person picking. The project’s goal: simplified processes, shorter delivery times, and maximum transparency throughout the entire material flow. Particularly impressive: even truck unloading is fully automated – within just a few minutes, the pallets are ready for further processing. At the same time, modern picking stations and visual user guidance ensure efficient and employee-friendly processes. An exciting project that demonstrates how well-designed automation and collaborative partnerships make future-proof intralogistics possible. 🤝 automation intralogistics warehousesolutions

Content: Automatic Weather Station (AWS) Assembly and Calibration Training for DNMG Staff – 2026 Hello Sobat PPSDM, 👋🌏 As part of efforts to strengthen the human resource capacity of DNMG Timor-Leste staff in meteorological instrumentation and calibration, the Automatic Weather Station (AWS) Assembly and Calibration Training was conducted from 16–26 June 2026 at the BMKG Headquarters, followed by a field practice session from 28 June–3 July 2026 at the Class III Kertajati Meteorological Station, Majalengka. This training was attended by five technicians from the Directorate of Meteorology and Geophysics (DNMG) Timor-Leste and delivered by instructors from BMKG’s Directorate of Instrumentation and Calibration. The program aims to enhance participants’ technical competencies in the assembly, installation, operation, maintenance, and calibration of Automatic Weather Stations (AWS) in accordance with established operational standards. Through this training, participants are expected to strengthen their technical capabilities in supporting reliable meteorological observation systems, ensuring that the data produced are more accurate, high-quality, and able to support meteorological services in Timor-Leste. Content: Cloud cost optimisation isn't a project. It's a discipline. Most growing companies set up their cloud infrastructure fast — and never go back. What we typically find in the first week of a Cloud Cost Audit: → Idle or over-sized compute instances → Unattached storage volumes → Reserved capacity purchased for workloads that moved → Dev/test environments running 24/7 For a mid-market eCommerce platform, we identified and eliminated $120K/year in waste — without any change to performance. Content: ⚛️ AWS, ASME, ASTM, API… od czego zacząć, jeśli chcesz lepiej zrozumieć standardy stosowane w projektach energetycznych i jądrowych na całym świecie? Właśnie dlatego uczestniczymy w trzydniowym programie szkoleniowym organizowanym w siedzibie Urzędu Dozoru Technicznego w Warszawie, poświęconym amerykańskim normom materiałowym i spawalniczym wykorzystywanym m.in. w sektorze energetyki jądrowej. Dla wielu polskich firm to cenna okazja do poznania systemu norm, który w wielu aspektach różni się od europejskich wymagań opartych na PED i normach EN. W wydarzeniu biorą udział przedstawiciele przemysłu, organizacji spawalniczych, jednostek inspekcyjnych oraz eksperci zainteresowani rozwojem kompetencji w obszarze międzynarodowych standardów technicznych. Content: You did everything right. Migrated to Databricks. Enabled Photon. Hit run. And your PySpark job… took the exact same time. 😶 Plot twist: Photon was never even running your code. Here's what's actually happening 👇 Photon is Databricks' blazing-fast, C++-based execution engine. It's vectorized, SIMD-optimized, and genuinely powerful — but only for native Spark SQL operations. The moment you drop a Python UDF into your pipeline? Spark bypasses Photon entirely. It serializes your data into Python objects, runs the function row by row in the Python runtime, then brings results back. All that overhead? Photon can't help. The culprit is hiding in plain sight inside your .withColumn() calls. The fix that changes everything: Replace Python UDFs → Native Spark SQL built-ins OR Arrow-based Pandas UDFs. That's it. 5 minutes of refactoring. 2–10x speed gains. This is the kind of thing that separates good data engineers from great ones — knowing why a tool isn't working, not just that it isn't. 📖 Full article with working code fix in bio link 👇 👉 Follow for weekly deep-dives into Spark, Databricks, and data engineering 🔥 PySpark Databricks DataEngineering ApacheSpark PhotonEngine BigDataAnalytics SparkOptimization PythonDeveloper DataPipeline TechTips DataScience LearnToCode

Content: ❄️ Snowflake Stages Explained in 6 Slides If you’ve worked with Snowflake, you’ve probably seen commands like: COPY INTO PUT GET But have you ever wondered where Snowflake actually reads the files from? 👉 The answer is Stages. A Stage acts as a bridge between your data files and Snowflake tables. Without Stages, loading large CSV, JSON, or Parquet files into Snowflake would be inefficient and difficult to manage. 🚀 In this carousel, you’ll learn: ✅ What a Stage is ✅ Why Stages are needed ✅ Types of Snowflake Stages ✅ How to create a Stage ✅ How COPY INTO works ✅ Real-world advantages of using Stages Types of Stages 🔹 User Stage (@~) 🔹 Table Stage (@%table_name) 🔹 Named Internal Stage 🔹 External Stage (AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCS) Why Stages Matter ⚡ Fast Bulk Loading ☁️ Cloud Storage Integration 🔒 Secure Data Transfer 📦 Supports CSV, JSON, Parquet, Avro, ORC 🔄 Reusable Across Multiple Pipelines 📤 Easy Data Export Real-World Flow Files ⬇️ Stage ⬇️ COPY INTO ⬇️ Snowflake Table ⬇️ Analytics & Insights 💡 Interview Question: Why can’t Snowflake directly load files from cloud storage? Because Snowflake’s bulk loading mechanism reads files through Stages using COPY INTO, providing scalability, security, and performance. 📌 Save this post for your next Snowflake interview. 🔄 Share with your Data Engineering team. 👇 Question: Which type of Stage do you use most often? 1️⃣ User Stage 2️⃣ Internal Stage 3️⃣ External Stage Follow .ai for more Snowflake, SQL, Data Engineering, AI, and MLOps content 🚀 Snowflake SnowflakeDB DataEngineering DataEngineer ETL ELT SQL CloudDataEngineering DataWarehouse AWS Azure GCP BigData AnalyticsEngineering DataPlatform DataArchitect LearnSnowflake DataAnalytics MLOps DataEngineerAI

Content: One of the biggest mistakes people make when exploring tech careers is assuming every data role does the same thing. They do not. Data Analytics, Data Science, and Data Engineering may all work with data, but they solve completely different problems. A Data Analyst helps businesses understand what happened and why. They turn raw information into reports, dashboards, and actionable insights that support decision making. A Data Scientist goes a step further. They use statistics, machine learning, and predictive models to answer questions about what is likely to happen next. A Data Engineer focuses on the foundation. They build the systems, pipelines, and infrastructure that make data available, reliable, and usable for everyone else. If you enjoy communicating insights and working closely with business teams, Data Analytics may be the right fit. If you enjoy statistics, machine learning, and predictive modelling, Data Science could be your path. If you enjoy building systems, solving technical challenges, and working behind the scenes, Data Engineering may be the best option. The good news? All three are in demand. The better news? You can learn all three at AMDOR Analytics. Not sure where to start? Send us a message and we’ll help you choose the right path. dataanalytics datascience dataengineering techcareers amdoranalytics

Content: 🇦🇺 Australia's Top News website rankings for May 2026 are here! A bit late from me after they were released this morning. held No1 with a 1.37m audience lead over at No2 and 🥁 was the 4th biggest news website in the country for the 6th month in a row. Thanks to the 7.2 million people who visited GdnAus in April! 🙏 In May just one of the Top10 news websites experienced an audience swing of more than 5%, and that was with a +12% audience lift, which helped them leapfrog to take the No7 spot. For most publishers in the Top20, it was quite a steady month for audiences visiting their website. The biggest audience loss, both in raw numbers and percentage, went to DailyMail, which was down 736k readers and -15% on April. It's a shock because in the past they've challenged for No3 and 4 in the rankings, but that result saw the DailyMail drop out of the Top10 news websites in Australia for the first time that I can remember. They now have half the audience in Ipsos that they had in March 2025. In April rankings I mentioned some other recent DailyMail falls, but definitely something to come back to. I'll also go through everything to see if I can find any month in the last 10yrs (so including pre-Ipsos Nielsen) where DailyMail hasn't been in the Top10. jumps 4 spots to 15th, CourierMail and DailyTele enter the Top20 as The Nightly, SkyNewsOz and Forbes drop out. ✨ Another record to mark. This is the first time in history that Guardian Australia has been ranked 4th or higher for six months in a row 👏 and it extends our record run to 11 consecutive months where Guardian Australia has been ranked 5th or higher. Historically we have held the No6 or 7 spot in perpetuity. audience media audiencedevelopment audienceengagement audiencegrowth seo search newsproduct product engagement

Content: What marketing KPIs should small businesses track? At MarkeThings, we focus on revenue, not vanity metrics. Here are 3 KPIs every SME should measure: 1. Marketing-Sourced Revenue How much revenue comes directly from your campaigns. Example: SEO landing pages generating $40k in quarterly sales. 2. Assisted Conversions Tracks channels that influence buyers before the final conversion. Example: Display ads contributing 30% of conversions later closed by Google Search. 3. Customer Lifetime Value (LTV) by Channel Shows which channels bring the highest long-term revenue. Example: Email subscribers generating 2.5x higher 12-month LTV than one-time paid ad customers. At MarkeThings, we combine SEO, paid ads, email marketing, server-side tracking, and multi-touch attribution to help SMEs across Australia, UK, Singapore, New Zealand, and Dubai measure real business growth. Because the metrics that matter most are: ✔ Revenue ✔ Profitability ✔ Retention ✔ Growth Not clicks. Not likes. Not impressions. Content: 📊 We tracked 1,600 posts to see what an influencer collab actually does to engagement. The data had a plot twist. 👇 We built a single "weighted engagement" score for every post → likes + (comments × 2), because a comment takes more effort than a like, so it should count more. Then we smoothed the noise with two lines: 📈 Rolling mean = the average engagement over a moving 30-day window (sensitive to viral outliers). 📉 Rolling median = the typical post in that same window (ignores the viral spikes; the more honest "normal day" line). The reveal? Engagement didn't peak during the March collab - it kept climbing and peaked in mid-June, ~3 months later 🤯. The collab window sits on the slope up, not the summit. Both the average AND the typical post agree on June, so this isn't just one viral post skewing things. Takeaway for brands: the impact of a collab isn't always instant and the spike you celebrate might not be the one that matters. Look at the trend, not the moment. 🔍 ⚠️ The fine print (because good data has caveats): • No follower data → we measured raw engagement, not engagement rate. If the audience grew, some of this shift is just reach, not performance. • Correlation ≠ causation → June could be a seasonal launch, another campaign, or sheer virality. We can't pin the peak on any single cause. • Hidden likes & outliers → a few mega-viral posts pull the mean hard; that's exactly why we plotted the median too. Data tells better stories when you respect what it can't say. 💬 What would you want us to analyze next? Content: Most brands don't have an influencer problem. 🤔 They have an outreach fatigue problem. Here's what usually happens: • Send 500 DMs. • Get 30 replies. • 20 ask for free products. • 7 disappear. • 2 post content. • 1 actually drives sales. Then founders conclude: 🙃 "Influencer marketing doesn't work." The reality? Most outreach systems are broken before the first message is sent. Common mistakes: ❌ Pulling creators from outdated databases. ❌ Sending the same generic pitch to everyone. ❌ Ignoring audience-brand fit. ❌ No follow-up process after the first DM. The brands getting results aren't necessarily contacting more creators. They're contacting better creators. With better qualification. And a structured follow-up process. Volume creates activity. Precision creates revenue. That's the difference. shopify dtc influencermarketing creatormarketing affiliatemarketingtips digitalmarketingstrategy

Content: "Vous pouvez ajouter de l'IA à notre app ?" On nous pose cette question 10 fois par mois. Et 9 fois sur 10, c'est la mauvaise question. La bonne, c'est : "Quel problème on veut résoudre pour l'utilisateur ?" Mettre de l'IA ne sert à rien. Mettre la bonne IA au bon endroit, c'est un multiplicateur. Voici ce qu'on peut intégrer dans les produits qu'on livre : → Moteur de matching personnalisé → Assistant conversationnel contextuel, pas un chatbot scripté → Auto-catégorisation et tagging → Résumé et extraction automatique → Génération assistée de contenu → Scoring et priorisation La règle qu'on s'impose : si l'IA n'apporte rien de concret à l'utilisateur final, on n'en met pas. Point. Et quand on en met : on documente ce qu'elle fait, on prévoit ses erreurs, on laisse l'utilisateur reprendre la main. Dans ton produit, ou celui que tu imagines, qu'est-ce que l'IA pourrait vraiment changer ? Pour en parler : lien en bio. IA Produit Startup DevWeb ProductManagement AgenceIA

Content: Cambiare strategia non è un segnale di fallimento, ma spesso il contrario: è un segnale di apprendimento. Nel percorso di una startup o di un progetto innovativo, è raro che la direzione iniziale resti quella giusta fino alla fine. Il mercato evolve, emergono nuovi bisogni e i dati aiutano a vedere ciò che all’inizio non era evidente. Insistere su una strategia che non funziona non è necessariamente determinazione, ma può diventare rigidità. Saper rivedere le proprie scelte, adattarsi e correggere la rotta significa aumentare le probabilità di arrivare a un reale product-market fit. Le realtà che crescono non sono quelle che non cambiano mai idea, ma quelle che imparano più velocemente quando e come farlo. 👉 E per te, cambiare strategia è un passo indietro o un passo avanti? Content: Your chatbot needs a personality. Not a gimmick -- a personality. THE PERSONALITY DESIGN FRAMEWORK: Step 1: DEFINE THE SPECTRUM Where does your brand fall? Formal (law firm, healthcare) to Casual (DTC brand, startup)? Step 2: CHOOSE A PERSONA The Professor: authoritative, precise, educational. Best for: finance, legal, healthcare. The Helpful Friend: warm, encouraging, conversational. Best for: e-commerce, SaaS, services. The Witty Assistant: playful, clever, personality-forward. Best for: DTC, media, lifestyle. Step 3: WRITE A VOICE GUIDE Document 10 example responses in your persona. How does your bot say "I don't know"? How does it celebrate? How does it apologize? Step 4: CONSISTENCY CHECK Every response should feel like it came from the same person. If your welcome message is casual but your error message is corporate, the personality breaks. Step 5: TEST WITH REAL USERS Ask 5 people: "What adjectives describe this chatbot?" If their words match your intended persona, you nailed it. Your chatbot's personality IS its brand. Design it intentionally. personality chatbot branding voice design conferbot ai saas tone copywriting ux cx tip education persona

Content: The difference between a workflow that runs and a workflow that survives production? Design discipline. A fintech ops team built a 140-node KYC pipeline in Autonoly. It ran perfectly for 11 months — through 3 API provider changes, 2 compliance updates, and a migration from Postgres to BigQuery. Not a single unplanned outage. When we asked how, their automation lead said: "We follow 10 rules religiously." Here they are: 1. One workflow, one purpose — scope creep kills reliability 2. Name every node descriptively — "transform_invoice_to_xero_format" beats "step_7" 3. Handle errors at every step — not just the happy path 4. Use variables, never hardcode — one config change updates 50 workflows 5. Test with real data before launch — synthetic data hides real-world edge cases 6. Set timeouts on all API calls — a hanging node can cascade into a full pipeline freeze 7. Log inputs and outputs — you cannot debug what you cannot see 8. Version your workflows — rollback should take 10 seconds, not 10 hours 9. Keep branches under 3 levels deep — complexity is technical debt with compound interest 10. Document the "why," not just the "what" — intent outlives implementation Rule 4 alone saved them 80+ hours of manual updates after their payment gateway switched endpoints. Which rule would you add to this list? Drop it below. Content: We recently convened the nasscom Growth Circle, bringing together founders and industry leaders to discuss how technology businesses can navigate evolving market realities, unlock growth opportunities, and create long-term value in the age of AI. The discussion highlighted a clear shift from AI experimentation to business outcomes, with organizations increasingly focused on ROI, operational efficiency, customer value, and sustainable growth. Key discussions from the session: • Leveraging domain expertise to guide customers through AI adoption and drive measurable business outcomes. • Strengthening value-based selling approaches and building customer trust through focused, high-impact wins. • Optimizing delivery through POD-based engineering models and operational efficiency frameworks. • Exploring Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT) models to capitalize on the growing GCC opportunity. • Building resilient businesses through stronger commercial models, talent strategies, and value realization frameworks. • Investing in reusable assets, scalable processes, and operational foundations to drive future readiness. A strong consensus emerged that sustainable growth will belong to organizations that successfully combine domain expertise, operational excellence, customer-centric innovation, and long-term resilience. We thank Maulik Bhansali, Founder, NetWeb Software and Chairperson, nasscom SME Council, for sharing his insights and facilitating the discussion. We also thank Amit Shah, Director, Axium Global and Member, nasscom SME Council, for enriching the session with his valuable perspectives. Content: Erstelle ein Ultrarealistisches Fashion-Porträt einer Frau basierend auf dem Referenzfoto. Die Gesichtsform, Augen, Lippen, Hautstruktur und die natürliche Ausstrahlung sollen möglichst originalgetreu erhalten bleiben. Die Frau läuft selbstbewusst durch einen sehr langen, dunklen Betontunnel direkt auf die Kamera zu. Am Ende des Tunnels ist nur ein kleiner, entfernter Lichtpunkt sichtbar, der Tiefe und Dramatik erzeugt. Der Tunnel wirkt endlos, düster und atmosphärisch. Die Frau blickt mit einem intensiven, starken und selbstsicheren Blick direkt in die Kamera. Der Fokus liegt besonders auf ihren Augen. Ihre Augen leuchten leicht, werden durch ein hochwertiges Editorial-Make-up betont: feiner Smokey-Eye-Look, perfekt definierte Wimpern, dezenter Eyeliner, sanfte Highlights um die Augenpartie und natürliche Haut mit hochwertigem Beauty-Retusche-Look Sie trägt ein oversized Long-Seidenhemd in dunklem Schokoladenbraun, luxuriöser fließender Stoff mit natürlichem Glanz. Eine Seite des Hemdes rutscht lässig über die Schulter. Dazu trägt sie enge schwarze Leggings. Beide Hände befinden sich entspannt in den Hosentaschen. Die Haare sind offen, locker fallend, leicht gewellt und bewegen sich dezent beim Gehen. Ihre Lippen wirken natürlich voll mit einem dezenten Gloss-Finish. Von der rechten Seite des Tunnels fällt ein schmaler Lichtstrahl ein, der einen sanften prismatischen Regenbogeneffekt erzeugt. Die Regenbogenfarben sind subtil, elegant und scheinen die Frau leicht zu umgeben, ohne künstlich zu wirken. Dramatische Kinobeleuchtung, dunkle Atmosphäre, nasser Tunnelboden mit Spiegelungen, geringe Tiefenschärfe, extrem detaillierte Hautstruktur, fotorealistische Augenreflexe, hochwertige Modefotografie, Vogue Editorial Style, Leica SL2-S, 85mm Objektiv, f/1.4, ultra realistic, hyper detailed, cinematic lighting, volumetric light, HDR, masterpiece, 8k, photorealitic chatgpttrend chatgptprompts instapic chatgpt

Content: Stop treating AI like a fancy chat box and start treating it like a strategic partner. 🤖 The real breakthrough in productivity isn't found in mastering the "perfect prompt." Instead, it’s found in building repeatable workflows and persistent AI agents that can manage complex, multi-step business processes. The true value of AI lies in augmentation, not replacement. When we stop viewing technology as a competitor and start seeing it as a collaborator, we unlock a level of scale that was previously impossible. To do this effectively, we must define a clear division of labor: ✅ Let AI handle the complex reasoning. ✅ Let automation take over repetitive execution. ✅ Reserve high-level strategy and genuine human connection for your team. By letting technology handle the heavy lifting of data and routine tasks, you free your people to do what they do best—lead, innovate, and connect. This is how we move from simply "using AI" to truly evolving alongside it. 👇 **How are you shifting your focus from simple prompts to building integrated AI workflows in your business? Content: Anthropic just put a spy in your Slack. A smart one. Claude Tag is now sitting in your workspace, reading every message, learning your company's language, your internal logic, your unwritten rules. It's always on. It's always listening. Most people will use it to summarize threads and draft replies. Fine. That's the surface layer. But here's what actually matters: Anthropic is building a memory system for every enterprise on the planet. Every Slack workspace that adopts this becomes a training ground for organizational context. Claude doesn't just know what GPT-4 knows. It knows *your* company. That's the moat they're building. Not the model. The context. For builders, this is the signal: the next wave of valuable AI products isn't about raw intelligence. It's about who has the deepest context. Your internal docs. Your customer history. Your team's reasoning patterns. If you're building for B2B, stop thinking about features. Start thinking about what context you can capture that nobody else can touch. The companies that win won't have the best AI. They'll have AI that knows the most about their specific world. Claude Tag is Anthropic's bet that the best place to capture that knowledge is where work actually happens. Slack, every day, all day. Hard to argue with that logic. Question for builders: are you designing your products to get smarter over time through user context — or are you just wrapping an API? claudecode aiagents buildwithAI llm aitools artificialintelligence

Content: Prompt engineering improves the first response. Loop engineering improves the final outcome. As AI coding agents take on more complex software tasks, a strong prompt is no longer enough. Real projects require agents to work through a structured cycle: Plan → Search → Modify → Verify → Repair → Summarise This is the idea behind Loop Engineering: designing AI workflows where agents use tests, logs, compiler errors, code reviews, and human feedback to continuously improve their work. A well-designed loop helps teams: • Reduce unverified AI-generated code • Make smaller, reversible changes • Detect wrong assumptions early • Improve reliability through continuous validation • Keep humans in control of product and architectural decisions The next generation of AI-enabled teams will not compete only on who writes better prompts. They will compete on who designs better loops. Build systems, not just prompts. Content: Voice agents are about to feel different. Nvidia Nemotron ASR Streaming is the new listening layer for any ai voice agent stack. Real time transcription with cache aware FastConformer RNNT architecture, which means it does not waste cycles re-listening to audio it has already processed. The result is lower latency, cleaner transcripts, and a voice product that feels like a conversation instead of a walkie talkie. Two variants ship out of the box. English only for tight latency on en US workloads. Multilingual for 40 language locales with automatic language detection in a single checkpoint. That retires the per language ASR routing layer most global support teams have been stitching together for years. Why this matters right now. ASR latency compounds through every step of a voice agent. Planner, action layer, text to speech, transport. Shave time off the listening layer and the entire interaction speeds up. Multilingual coverage is no longer optional. The multilingual variant collapses a routing problem into a model call. Deployment is a NIM container, not a six month infrastructure project. What a serious rollout looks like. Pick the variant for your language coverage. Smoke test on real production audio. Measure microphone to first token latency, not just model latency. Wire the transcript into whatever consumes it next. Planner, summarizer, CRM, downstream LLM. Monitor word error rate, latency percentiles, and task success. Optimize against task success. Teams that anchor their voice strategy on a streaming first ASR engine this year will have a head start. Voice is finally ready to feel real. Content: A AWS lançou o Lambda MicroVMs, recurso baseado em Firecracker que permite executar ambientes Linux isolados por até 8 horas, com preservação de estado, suspensão e retomada sob demanda. A novidade amplia o uso do Lambda para tarefas mais longas, como execução de código não confiável, análise de vulnerabilidades, workloads de CI/CD, sessões interativas e agentes de inteligência artificial. O recurso oferece uma alternativa serverless para ambientes temporários e isolados, mas sua adoção exige atenção a permissões, rede, IAM, logs e controle de acesso, especialmente quando usado com agentes de IA capazes de executar comandos e acessar ferramentas corporativas. No lançamento, o Lambda MicroVMs está disponível em regiões selecionadas da AWS e na arquitetura ARM64 com Graviton. Para ler a notícia completa acesse: cybersecbrazil.com.br CyberSecurity InfoSec CloudSecurity AWS Serverless Lambda Firecracker Cibersegurança Tecnologia InteligênciaArtificial DevSecOps

Content: ☸️ Most ML engineers avoid Kubernetes because it looks intimidating. But avoiding it doesn't make it go away, it just makes you dependent on someone else who understands it. Here's Kubernetes for ML in 5 minutes 👇 Docker runs one container. Kubernetes runs hundreds of containers reliably, with scaling, self-healing, and automation built in. Why ML workloads need K8s: ⚡ GPU-intensive → Resource requests & limits prevent resource starvation 📈 Traffic spikes → Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales automatically 💾 Persistent storage → Volumes survive pod restarts ⏰ Scheduled retraining → CronJobs automate recurring workflows The 4 Kubernetes objects that cover most ML deployments: • Pod → Runs your container • Deployment → Manages replicas & updates • Service → Stable endpoint for your app • Ingress → Routes external traffic Flow: Ingress → Service → Deployment → Pods One lesson most tutorials skip: Always define resource requests and limits. Without them: ❌ Autoscaling becomes unreliable ❌ One workload can consume an entire node ❌ Performance becomes unpredictable And then there's Helm. Instead of managing dozens of YAML files, Helm lets you template once and deploy consistently across environments. You don't need to master Kubernetes overnight. Start with managed platforms like GKE, EKS, or AKS and focus on your ML workloads first. What's holding your team back from Kubernetes adoption, complexity, cost, or time to learn? 🎓 Want hands-on experience with Kubernetes, DevOps, MLOps, AIOps, and SRE on AWS? Join the 28-Week Live Bootcamp: MLOps Kubernetes K8s MachineLearning MLEngineer DevOps Helm CloudNative AIOps AWS

Content: 🌍 𝗔𝗪𝗦 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗜𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝟱 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀 Understanding AWS Global Infrastructure is essential for building applications that are secure, scalable, highly available, and fast for users worldwide. 𝗜𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘃𝗶𝘀𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗴𝘂𝗶𝗱𝗲, 𝘄𝗲 𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸 𝗱𝗼𝘄𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗪𝗦 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗻𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀: ✅ 𝗥𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 – Geographic AWS locations such as Mumbai, Hyderabad, Virginia, and London. ✅ 𝗔𝘃𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗭𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 (𝗔𝗭𝘀) – Isolated data centers inside a Region designed for high availability. ✅ 𝗘𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 – Help deliver cached content faster using services like CloudFront. ✅ 𝗟𝗼𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗭𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 – Bring compute and storage closer to metro cities for low-latency workloads. ✅ 𝗪𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗵 𝗭𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 – Support ultra-low latency applications over 5G networks. ✅ 𝗔𝗪𝗦 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 – Extend AWS infrastructure and services into your on-premises data center. 𝗔 𝘀𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹-𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗹𝘂𝗱𝗲: ➡️Users → CloudFront → Application Load Balancer → EC2 across Multiple AZs → RDS Multi-AZ → S3 This design improves: 🔹 Application availability 🔹 Fault tolerance 🔹 Disaster recovery readiness 🔹 Performance and low latency 🔹 Scalability for growing workloads 𝗙𝗼𝗿 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀, 𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗸𝗲𝘆 𝗽𝗼𝗶𝗻𝘁: 𝗥𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗼𝗻 = Geographic Area 𝗔𝘃𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗭𝗼𝗻𝗲 = Isolated infrastructure inside a Region Mastering these concepts helps you make better architecture decisions in AWS certifications, interviews, and real-world projects. 🚀 𝗙𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗼𝘄 𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻 𝗣𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗱𝗮𝗶𝗹𝘆 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗰𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆: 𝗝𝗼𝗶𝗻 𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁𝘀𝗔𝗽𝗽 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆: 𝗪𝗲𝗯𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗲: AWS CloudComputing AWSGlobalInfrastructure AWSRegions AvailabilityZones CloudFront AWSOutposts CloudArchitecture DevOps CloudEngineering AWSCertification VyomanantAcademy

Content: 📅 Dia 07 de 30 Desafio de dados Databricks Professional ✨ Managed vs. External Tables: Onde seu dado realmente mora? ​Fechando a primeira semana do desafio com chave de ouro! Hoje o foco foi entender o impacto físico e o ciclo de vida dos dados no S3/ADLS. A diferença entre uma tabela Managed e uma External vai muito além da sintaxe do código — ela define quem é o dono do dado e o que acontece quando você roda um comando fatal. ​📌 Managed Tables (Tabelas Gerenciadas) ​O conceito: O Databricks gerencia tanto os metadados no Unity Catalog quanto os arquivos físicos no cloud storage. ​O perigo: Se você executar um DROP TABLE, o Unity Catalog apaga o registro da tabela E deleta permanentemente os arquivos físicos no S3/ADLS. ​Quando usar: Ideal para tabelas onde o Databricks tem controle total do ciclo de vida (como nas camadas Silver e Gold internas). ​📌 External Tables (Tabelas Externas) ​O conceito: Você define um caminho específico via LOCATION. O Databricks passa a gerenciar apenas os metadados. ​A segurança: Se você rodar um DROP TABLE, apenas o ponteiro/registro no Unity Catalog é apagado. Os seus arquivos físicos continuam intactos no storage. ​Quando usar: Essencial quando outras ferramentas externas precisam acessar os mesmos arquivos ou quando você precisa garantir que o dado bruto jamais será deletado por acidente. ​✨ Reflexão Diária Entender o ciclo de vida físico do dado evita aquele frio na barriga clássico de sumir com Terabytes de produção por engano. Uma boa governança começa sabendo exatamente quem manda no ciclo de vida da sua infraestrutura. ​💫💻📚🌸🎯 📌 Portfólio: ericaignatios.com.br 🌸 Blog: kirabyte.com.br 📸 Instagram: 🎥 Youtube: 💻 GitHub: github.com/ericaabrantes ​estudos dados engenhariadedados databricks unitycatalog lakehouse cloudstorage pyspark

Content: Your Delta Live Tables pipeline was doing completely fine… until it suddenly wasn’t. 🤯 We’ve all been there. You deploy a beautiful DLT pipeline in Databricks. It runs flawlessly on your test datasets. It even handles early production data like a charm. But then, real-world data scaling kicks in. Data volume spikes, schema drift happens, or an unoptimized stateful stream bogs down the entire engine. Suddenly, your "automated" pipeline requires constant manual restarts. 📉 It’s not bad luck; it’s a design bottleneck. To build resilient data infrastructure, you have to look under the hood of how DLT handles schema evolution, memory management, and data quality rules under heavy load. Content: Your AI agents don’t need perfect data. They need context. 🧠💻 Trying to build a flawless data warehouse is a quick way to burn out your data engineering team. Real ground truth shifts constantly, and column-level alerts quickly turn into noisy pipelines nobody pays attention to. The solution? Risk-Based Data Quality Tiering. 🎯 By categorizing your data from Tier 0 (regulatory and revenue-critical tables backed by strict code-based contracts) to Tier 3 (one-off scratch tables), you protect what matters most without slowing down innovation. 🛑 The biggest trap? Tier creep. If everything is labeled Tier 0, nothing is. Check out the link in our bio to read the framework modern data teams are using to scale their infrastructure for AI without drowning in paperwork. Content: If you're chasing trends, you're already late. One of the biggest mistakes brands make in influencer marketing is believing that going viral means jumping on every trend. It doesn't. By the time a trend is identified, approved, briefed, produced, and published, the audience has often already moved on. That's why so many trend-based campaigns fail. Not because the content is bad. Not because the creators are wrong. Because the timing is. The brands that consistently generate results don't chase trends. They identify the right trends early, evaluate whether they actually fit their audience, and activate the right creators before the opportunity disappears. And there's another mistake many brands make: Assuming every viral trend is relevant to their business. A trend with millions of views means nothing if it doesn't align with your audience, your product, or your brand positioning. In influencer marketing, relevance almost always beats reach. The best-performing campaigns happen when the right trend, the right creator, and the right audience meet at the right moment. Virality isn't random. It's built. What's the biggest trend-jacking mistake you've seen brands make? Let us know in the comments. And if you're a brand looking to build influencer marketing strategies that go beyond simply chasing trends, contact our team through the link in bio. ⬆️ InfluencerMarketing TrendMarketing CreatorMarketing

Content: The workflow ran flawlessly for 47 days. 14,000+ successful executions. Zero issues. On day 48, at 3:42 AM, a payment gateway returned a 503 instead of a 200. Without error handling: the entire pipeline crashed silently. No Slack alert. No retry. No fallback. Nobody noticed for 72 hours. 4,200 customer invoices were never processed. The team found out from a customer complaint — not from their own monitoring. With error handling: the node retried 3 times with exponential backoff (2s, 4s, 8s). On the third failure, it triggered a fallback path that routed to a backup payment processor. Simultaneously, a Slack alert hit the on-call channel in 12 seconds. Failed records were queued in a dead letter queue for manual review. Total customer impact: zero. 5 error handling patterns every production workflow needs: 1. Retry with exponential backoff — 73% of transient API failures self-resolve within 3 attempts 2. Fallback/alternative paths — if the primary service is down, route to a backup 3. Dead letter queues — failed items go somewhere recoverable, not into the void 4. Real-time alert notifications — Slack, PagerDuty, email. Within seconds, not days 5. Graceful degradation — processing 9,800 of 10,000 records is infinitely better than processing zero Autonoly has all 5 built into the workflow builder. Every node supports retry logic, error branching, and alert triggers. No custom code required. Stop building for the happy path. Build for day 48. Content: 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗜 𝗗𝗼𝗻’𝘁 𝗔𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗪𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗛𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 "𝗩𝗶𝗯𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴" Lately, I've seen a lot of developers criticizing "vibe coding" and AI-assisted development as if it's somehow destroying software engineering. I see it differently. The problem isn't the tools. The problem is how people use them. A calculator didn't kill mathematics. GitHub didn't kill programming. Frameworks didn't kill software engineering. AI won't either. What AI does is amplify the capabilities of the person using it. If someone lacks understanding of architecture, databases, security, scalability, debugging, or software design principles, AI will simply help them make mistakes faster. But for experienced developers who understand the fundamentals, AI becomes a force multiplier. Over the last few months, I've shipped hundreds of projects ranging from SaaS platforms, digital products, business applications, web portals, mobile applications, dashboards, and desktop software. AI-assisted development didn't replace my expertise it accelerated execution. Instead of spending hours on repetitive boilerplate code, I spend more time solving business problems, improving user experiences, refining architecture, and delivering products faster. The reality is that clients don't pay for how many lines of code you write. They pay for solutions. They pay for products that work. They pay for results. The developers who will thrive in the coming years won't be the ones fighting AI. They'll be the ones learning how to combine technical expertise, product thinking, and AI tools to build better software faster than ever before. AI is not replacing developers. Developers who know how to leverage AI effectively are replacing those who refuse to adapt. What's your take on AI-assisted development and vibe coding? Content: Todo mês a mesma ansiedade: quanto vai vir na fatura esse mês? Hora extra aqui, chamado fora do escopo ali, reunião que virou projeto, projeto que virou cobrança extra. O backoffice tradicional funciona assim — e você nunca sabe o número real até o boleto chegar. A gente construiu um modelo diferente. Fee fixo. Todo mês. Sem surpresa, sem negociação na última hora, sem "ah, mas isso não estava no contrato". Você sabe exatamente o que vai pagar antes de fechar o mês — e o que vai receber em troca. Isso muda completamente a forma como um CFO ou diretor consegue planejar. Não é só uma questão de conforto. É previsibilidade de caixa. É poder olhar para o próximo trimestre sem uma variável descontrolada no meio do custo operacional. Quando o backoffice vira um serviço gerenciado com escopo claro, agentes de IA executando o operacional e integrações rodando sem depender de pessoa física para cada tarefa manual, o custo deixa de ser imprevisível e passa a ser estratégico. Previsível não significa barato. Significa controlável. E controle é o que separa operação de gestão. 🎯 Se o seu backoffice ainda funciona no modelo "vamos ver o que vem", talvez seja hora de repensar a estrutura toda. gestaofinanceira automacaoempresarial backoffice financeirodigital saas

Content: 99% de SLA não é só número bonito em contrato. É a diferença entre um backoffice que você consegue cobrar e um que você torce pra funcionar. A maioria das empresas opera no escuro. Tarefas críticas dependem de pessoas que ficam doentes, saem de férias, pedem demissão. E quando algo para, você só descobre depois que o estrago já foi feito. 🔍 Quando o operacional roda com agentes de IA, muda o jogo: cada processo tem tempo de execução medido, cada entrega tem log, cada falha gera alerta imediato. Não é promessa — é dado. É o mesmo princípio que nuvens enterprise usam pra garantir disponibilidade de infraestrutura, aplicado agora ao seu backoffice financeiro, fiscal e operacional. CFOs estão começando a exigir isso. Não por modismo, mas porque finalmente é possível. Você consegue abrir um painel e ver: conciliação bancária executada em X minutos, relatório de fechamento entregue no prazo, integrações com ERP rodando sem intervenção humana. Isso é gestão de verdade. Visibilidade real. Responsabilidade mensurável. 📊 Se o seu fornecedor de operações não consegue te mostrar o SLA dele, talvez o problema não seja a equipe — seja a falta de estrutura pra medir. Content: 💡 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 "𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗗𝗼 𝗜 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗔𝗜?" 𝘁𝗼 "𝗦𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝗜 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗔𝗜?" — 𝗠𝗿. 𝗩𝗶𝗻𝗮𝘆 𝗕𝗮𝗯𝘂 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗮, 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝗲𝘀 𝗖𝗣𝗠𝗔𝗜 Most AI projects fail. Not because the technology breaks—because the business thinking breaks first. 𝗠𝗿. 𝗩𝗶𝗻𝗮𝘆 𝗕𝗮𝗯𝘂 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗮, 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗿𝗲𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘆 & 𝗠𝗔𝗥𝗖𝗢𝗠 𝗼𝗳 𝗣𝗠𝗜𝗣𝗖𝗖, shared a pattern organizations repeat: ✗ Cool demo launched ✗ Stakeholders excited ✗ Pilot begins ✗ No adoption ✗ Project dies 𝗠𝗿. 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗮 𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗸𝗲 𝗱𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗹𝘆 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗲: Organizations ask the wrong question at the start. They ask "How can we use AI?" instead of "Should we use AI here at all?" With 16+ years translating enterprise strategy into scaled execution, Mr. Tarala introduced CPMAI—Cognitive Project Management for AI. The message was clear: 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗿 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝘀. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲. 𝗛𝗲 𝘄𝗮𝗹𝗸𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗣𝗠𝗔𝗜 𝗟𝗶𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘆𝗰𝗹𝗲: 1️⃣ 𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Why? (Not "how impressive is the tech?") 2️⃣ 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Do we have usable, trustworthy data? 3️⃣ 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Can AI actually solve this problem? 4️⃣ 𝗘𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 — Does it work well enough? 5️⃣ 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 — Will people actually use it? 6️⃣ 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 — Is it still creating measurable business value? 𝗠𝗿. 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗹𝗮'𝘀 𝗚𝗼𝗹𝗱𝗲𝗻 𝗥𝘂𝗹𝗲: AI Success = Business + Data + Technology + Adoption He grounded it with a real example: PMIPCC is building an AI Member Assistant because 100 members ask the same 5 questions every week. The leverage point was obvious. The framework made it safe and scalable. 𝗛𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵𝘁: Strategy without execution fails. Execution without intelligence stalls. Intelligence without governance scales the wrong things. The room left understanding: 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗮 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗲𝗿, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗿. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲. 𝗚𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝘆. Content: Most companies waste hours every week on documents that AI could process automatically. Mistral OCR 4 is not just another OCR tool. It returns structured data: where text sits on the page, what type of block it is, and a confidence score for each extraction. That is exactly what AI agents need to take reliable action on invoices, contracts, forms, and reports. What this makes possible: Invoices processed without per-supplier templates. Contracts reviewed in minutes instead of hours. Multilingual documents handled by a single pipeline across 170 languages. Company knowledge that is actually searchable with source citations. Compliance workflows with built-in audit trails. The key design decision: use confidence thresholds as your workflow gate. High confidence extractions flow automatically. Low confidence ones go to human review. You get speed where accuracy is high and safety where it is not. The companies building document automation infrastructure now will have a significant operational advantage as the space matures. Content: Begint je AI-agent vaker fouten te maken? Dan denk je misschien dat prompt engineering de oplossing is. Maar het probleem zit eigenlijk ergens anders: Er is een vaardigheid die veel meer impact heeft en waar nog weinig over gesproken wordt, namelijk context engineering. De twee klinken gelijkaardig, maar ze spelen zich op een heel ander niveau af. ➡ Prompt engineering gaat alleen over hoe je je prompt opstelt. ➡ Context engineering gaat over alles wat een AI-agent weet gedurende een volledige werksessie, en hoe je dat actief beheert. Moderne AI-modellen hebben een context window van zo'n 200.000 tokens. Dat klinkt ruim, tot je ziet hoe snel die volloopt. Een agent die tegelijk je codebase analyseert, tools aanroept, gesprekshistoriek bijhoudt en instructies onthoudt, vult die ruimte sneller dan je denkt. Veel developers zullen het gevoel herkennen. Je werkt al een tijdje met je agent, en code die hij een halfuur geleden nog correct schreef, zit nu vol fouten. Hij lijkt de instructies te vergeten die je aan het begin van de sessie meegaf. Dus pas je je prompt aan, maar het wordt er niet beter op. Het voelt alsof de tool gewoon onbetrouwbaar is, maar eigenlijk zit de context window gewoon te vol om nog goed te kunnen redeneren. Er is een drempel, ruwweg 40% van de context window, waarna de kwaliteit merkbaar daalt. Ontwikkelaars noemen dat de "Dumb Zone". De meeste mensen weten niet dat die drempel bestaat, en werken er daardoor de hele tijd tegenaan zonder te begrijpen wat er misgaat. De oplossing zit in begrijpen wat er in die context window zit, wat eruit moet, en hoe je een agent structureert zodat hij gefocust blijft. Een andere prompt schrijven lost het probleem niet op. We schreven er een uitgebreide blog over met concrete technieken om je context window goed te beheren. 👉 Lees het op onze blog. Link in bio. ContextEngineering AIAgents AIDrivenDevelopment

Content: Manual competitor tracking is a quiet productivity killer. Most growth teams waste hours every week bookmarking pricing pages, checking changelogs, and copy-pasting screenshots into stale spreadsheets. Here is how to automate the entire loop in under an hour using a simple no-code stack (Make + Clay + OpenAI): 1. Detection (Make): Set up a daily trigger to ping competitor change logs, pricing tables, or homepages. 2. Extraction (Clay): Fetch the updated HTML and compare it against the snapshot from the previous day. 3. Analysis (GPT-4o): Feed the raw HTML differences into an LLM with a highly specific prompt: "Ignore minor structural script changes. Extract actual changes in feature positioning, pricing plans, or messaging." 4. Delivery (Slack/Teams): Push clean, structured bullet-point summaries directly to your GTM channel. Instead of manual browsing, your sales and product teams get market intelligence updates on autopilot. Live competitive awareness, zero manual scraping. What workflows are you automating to keep tabs on your market? AIAgents nocode marketingautomation growth productivity

Content: Your AI has six doors into your business. Most companies have only ever locked the front one. When most people think about AI security, they think about keeping attackers out. Firewalls. Authentication. Access controls. The front door. But AI systems don't operate through a single door. They interact with users, consume data, connect to tools, store context, integrate with third-party systems, and influence business processes. Each of those becomes a potential entry point. Here are six doors every organization should be paying attention to: 1. The Prompt Door Anyone who can interact with your AI can attempt to influence its behavior. 2. The Data Door AI systems learn from and rely on the information they receive. Poor or manipulated data can create poor outcomes. 3. The Tool Door Every API, database, email platform, or business system connected to AI expands its ability to take action. 4. The Memory Door What your AI remembers can be just as important as what it knows. Retained context creates both value and risk. 5. The Integration Door Third-party applications, plugins, and connectors often introduce risks organizations never evaluate. 6. The Human Door Employees remain one of the largest attack surfaces. Shadow AI usage, unmanaged tools, and poor practices can bypass even the strongest technical controls. The mistake many organizations make is securing the model while ignoring the ecosystem around it. But attackers rarely target the strongest point They look for the easiest door. The question isn't: "Is our AI secure?" The better question is: "Which of these six doors is currently open?" Because AI security isn't about protecting a model. It's about securing every pathway that can influence its decisions and actions. 📌 Save this post and use it as a checklist during your next AI security review. Content: One of the biggest automation mistakes I see is jumping to the solution too early. How much does a palletiser cost? How much does a capping machine cost? What's the budget? They're all reasonable questions. The problem is that they're often asked before we've fully understood what needs to be achieved. What product? What throughput? What level of flexibility? What labour challenges are we trying to solve? What does success actually look like? It's tempting to jump straight to solutions and budgets. But that's where projects can start on the wrong foot. The wrong assumptions get made. The wrong expectations get set. And sometimes the wrong solution gets pursued altogether. The best automation projects I've been involved in all started the same way. Not with a machine. Not with a budget. With a problem. Understand the problem properly and the right solution usually becomes much easier to find. automation manufacturing engineering industrialautomation problemsolving processevolution

Content: These are 9 DevOps mistakes that show up repeatedly in startups. Some are expensive. Some are embarrassing. A few can take down entire systems. 1. One AWS account for everything Dev, staging, and production in the same account creates unnecessary risk. A dev misconfiguration can directly impact production or cost. Separate accounts early. 2. No billing alerts Without cost monitoring, cloud spend becomes invisible until it’s too late. A single Auto Scaling misconfiguration once triggered 50 instances instead of 5 for days. Result: a $12,000 bill. 3. Over-engineering before launch Kubernetes, service meshes, and complex infrastructure before product-market fit. Most early-stage systems fail from complexity, not lack of scale. 4. Hardcoded AWS credentials Embedding credentials in code or repositories is a critical security risk. Secrets should be managed properly from day one (not “later”). 5. Public SSH access (0.0.0.0/0 on port 22) Exposing SSH to the internet guarantees exposure to automated brute-force attacks. Session Manager or restricted access should be the default. 6. Deploying on Friday afternoons Production changes close to weekends increase risk and slow response time if something breaks. Deployment timing is part of reliability strategy. 7. No rollback strategy Every deployment should answer one question: How is this reverted in minutes if it breaks production? Without that, deployments are high-risk events. 8. No monitoring before launch Monitoring after an outage is reactive, not operational maturity. CloudWatch alarms and health checks should exist before production traffic. 9. Single point of failure everywhere Single EC2, single AZ, single NAT Gateway introduces avoidable downtime risk. Systems should assume components will fail not behave perfectly. Most production incidents are not caused by complex failures. They come from basic infrastructure decisions made too early or too casually. Strong DevOps practice is often just avoiding these fundamentals. devops aws cloudcomputing sre systemdesign

Content: Patch day. Two words that can trigger anything from mild annoyance to full-blown panic depending on how old your infrastructure is. Most teams don't intentionally neglect updates. They're busy shipping features, onboarding customers and fixing production issues. Updating third-party infrastructure components often ends up somewhere at the bottom of the backlog, right next to all those things everyone agrees are important but nobody has time for. The problem is that technical debt compounds. Maybe your Kubernetes version is a few releases behind. Your Helm charts haven't been updated in months. That Terraform provider still works alright, so why check it? Then eventually the day comes when you actually need to upgrade something and suddenly you're not upgrading one version. You're upgrading ten or more. I've seen this happen more than once. What should have been a routine maintenance task turned into a project spanning several weeks because too many dependencies had drifted too far apart. The longer you delay, the more painful it becomes. That's why I'm a big fan of continuous upgrades. Not because upgrading software is exciting. It isn't. But reviewing ten small changes every week is significantly easier than reviewing three hundred changes once per year. Tools like Renovate help automate a large part of that process. Instead of manually checking every dependency, container image, Terraform provider or Helm chart, Renovate continuously scans for updates and creates pull requests automatically. You still stay in control. Nothing gets deployed without review, but your team no longer has to remember what needs updating and when. The result is usually not only just better security, but also less operational risk. Because patch days stop being special events. They become part of normal operations. How are you currently handling updates for third-party infrastructure components? Are you already using Renovate or are upgrades still mostly manual? Content: ⚡ Most data engineers use Spark every day — but freeze when asked: "What does the Driver actually do?" Here's the truth nobody told you clearly: Spark has a "Brain" and a "Muscle." 🧠 DRIVER = The Brain → Converts your code into a DAG (execution plan) → Schedules tasks across the cluster → Tracks which executor is alive and which crashed → Never processes a single row of data itself 💪 EXECUTOR = The Muscle → Actually runs your transformations & actions → Caches data in memory for speed → Sends heartbeats to the Driver every few seconds → Lives on worker nodes across the cluster Here's what nobody talks about: If your Driver dies → your entire job dies. It holds the SparkContext. If one Executor dies → Spark just reruns that task elsewhere. No drama. Knowing this one thing changes how you debug, tune, and architect every Spark application you'll ever write. I wrote a deep-dive article that explains the whole Driver–Executor duo in a way that will actually stick. 📖 Link in bio → Medium: Spark Driver vs Executor 🔔 Follow for weekly content on Data Engineering, Spark, and Big Data that cuts through the noise. ▶️ Subscribe to my YouTube for visual walkthroughs → ApacheSpark DataEngineering SparkTutorial PySpark BigDataEngineer LearnDataEngineering TechTips DataEngineerLife SparkArchitecture Databricks 100DaysOfCode

Content: 📅 Dia 08 de 30 do Desafio de dados Databricks Professional ✨ Auto Loader Master: Schema Evolution e Detecção de Drift ​Abrindo a segunda semana do desafio com o pé no acelerador em Ingestão Escalável e Streaming! Hoje o foco total foi dominar o Auto Loader e aprender a lidar com uma das maiores dores de cabeça na engenharia de dados: a mudança repentina na estrutura dos arquivos que chegam no Data Lake. ​📌 O Poder do Auto Loader (cloudFiles) ​Ingestão Incremental Eficiente: Ele descobre novos arquivos assim que eles chegam no S3/ADLS de forma absurdamente eficiente, usando notificação de arquivos ou listagem otimizada, sem precisar reprocessar o que já passou. ​Schema Evolution (Evolução de Esquema): Em vez de o pipeline quebrar no meio da madrugada porque uma nova coluna surgiu, o Auto Loader consegue detectar a mudança e evoluir o schema automaticamente (cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode), adaptando a tabela Delta sem intervenção manual. ​Detecção de Schema Drift com _rescued_data: Se uma coluna nova aparecer ou um tipo de dado mudar drasticamente, o Auto Loader joga essa informação para uma coluna especial de "dados resgatados". Isso garante que o pipeline continue rodando e nenhum dado seja perdido enquanto você analisa a mudança. ​✨ Reflexão Diária No mundo real, os dados são vivos e mudam o tempo todo. Um pipeline verdadeiramente resiliente não é aquele que assume que o dado sempre virá perfeito, mas sim aquele que sabe se adaptar ao "drift" de forma inteligente e segura. Content: Why the data system your company built for basic reporting is quietly failing your AI. 🛑🖥️ Most corporations organize their modern data lakes using a pattern called the "Medallion Architecture," which filters raw info through three separate steps (Bronze, Silver, and Gold). While this is great for basic charts and graphs, it creates a massive blind spot for advanced AI features like search bots and automated assistants. When an AI model hallucinated or retrieves the wrong document piece, your developers need to know exactly where and when that individual row of data was corrupted. Traditional systems can only track large groups of tables, making finding a single broken record look like searching for a needle in a haystack. Upgrading to Apache Iceberg v3 introduces native Row Lineage. This smart update tags every single row with its own digital passport (_row_id), allowing your engineers to instantly query the life history of any data record with 100% precision. Stop trying to power tomorrow's AI on yesterday's blueprints. Learn how to architect elite, crystal-clear enterprise data platforms at DataEngineering ApacheIceberg DataOps

Content: Your first task as a data engineer at a new company isn't fixing the pipeline. Content: CASE STUDY: ONLINE COACH 📈 An online coach had a problem many businesses secretly struggle with. People were visiting her website every day. Traffic wasn't the issue. The real question was: **"If people are visiting my website, why aren't they buying?"** Hundreds of visitors would come. They would read about her programs. Browse her services. Then leave. No enquiry. No purchase. No follow-up. Then she realized something important: **Most people don't buy the first time they see your offer.** People need time. People need trust. People need reminders. So instead of trying to sell immediately, she changed her strategy. She created a simple **5-day email nurture sequence.** For five days, she stayed connected with her audience. 📩 Day 1: Welcome email and her story 📩 Day 2: Valuable tips and practical advice 📩 Day 3: Client success stories and testimonials 📩 Day 4: Solutions to common objections 📩 Day 5: A clear offer with a call to action She stopped chasing customers. She started building relationships. The results after just 30 days were impressive: 📧 48% email open rate 📈 27% increase in sales The biggest lesson? 💡 **Follow-up creates conversions.** Many businesses lose customers not because their product is bad, but because they stop the conversation too early. Remember this: ❌ Website visitors are not lost opportunities. ❌ People rarely buy the first time they see you. ✅ Consistent follow-up builds trust. ✅ Trust creates sales. The fortune is often in the follow-up. 👇 Be honest: Are you following up with your potential customers? Comment **YES** or **NO** below. 💾 Save this post and share it with a business owner who needs this reminder. Content: The game changed. The foundation didn't. Search used to be about ranking on Google. Now it's about being trusted by AI. But here's what most marketers are getting wrong: they're treating GEO (Generative Engine Optimisation) and SEO as a choice — pick one, skip the other. That's not how it works. → GEO without SEO means AI has no reason to trust you. No domain authority, no structured content, no backlink credibility — AI models skip right past you when forming answers. → SEO without GEO means you rank on Google, but you're invisible when someone asks ChatGPT, Perplexity, or Gemini a question your business should be answering. You don't get to choose one layer. Both need to run simultaneously, or you're already losing ground to competitors who figured this out. The brands winning in AI-era search built their SEO foundation first — then layered GEO on top: structured answers, cited content, E-E-A-T signals, semantic clarity. The question isn't SEO or GEO. It's: are you running both — and are they talking to each other? SEO GEO AIMarketing SearchStrategy GenerativeSearch

Content: Investor thua 18 lần vẫn thắng — bạn chỉ có 1 ván, vậy bạn đa dạng hóa rủi ro kiểu gì? 🎯 Đây là bất đối xứng lớn nhất giữa founder và investor mà ít ai chịu ngồi xuống phân tích thẳng thắn. Một VC có thể rót tiền vào 20 startup cùng lúc. Họ biết trước rằng 18 cái sẽ chết — và họ vẫn ngủ ngon vì chỉ cần 1 đến 2 cái thắng lớn là cả portfolio có lãi. ... Đó là đặc quyền của người chơi với portfolio. Nhưng founder không có đặc quyền đó. Bạn chỉ có 1 công ty duy nhất. Nếu nó chết, không có portfolio nào bù lại. Không có vòng tiếp theo để cân bằng. Vậy founder "đa dạng hóa rủi ro" bằng cách nào khi không thể đầu tư dàn trải như investor? Sau hơn 14 năm coaching và trực tiếp tham gia BOD tại VuaCua, Nina Ecom Center, VCO Group, HOPE Corp, tôi quan sát thấy 3 lớp bảo vệ mà phần lớn founder không chủ động xây — và thường chỉ nhận ra mình thiếu khi đã quá muộn. 1)Lớp đầu tiên là đa dạng dòng doanh thu. 💰 Khi 80% doanh thu đến từ 1 sản phẩm duy nhất hoặc 1 khách hàng duy nhất, bạn không đang kinh doanh — bạn đang đặt cược. Nguyên tắc quan trọng ở đây là dòng doanh thu mới phải tận dụng được năng lực cốt lõi sẵn có, không cần build lại từ đầu. Đây là bộ đệm, không phải sự phân tán. Mở thêm revenue stream mà không liên quan gì đến core competency thực ra còn nguy hiểm hơn — vì nó chia cắt sự tập trung mà không tạo ra bảo vệ thực sự. 2)Lớp thứ hai là đa dạng kênh phân phối. 📊 ... 📚 Muốn tiết kiệm 1-3 năm? 5 cuốn ebook thực chiến: 🔹Nhà Sáng Lập 01 Người Nhờ Cộng Tác Với AI Claude 🔹 Cách Định Giá Startup Idea → Series B từ A–Z 🔹 Cách Gọi Vốn Cho Startup Founder Idea → Series B 🔹 Cách Xây Dựng BOD Startup Để Gọi Vốn Thành Công 🔹 13 Bước Xây Dựng 01 Công Ty Nhiều Nhà Đầu Tư Muốn Rót Vốn Và Mua Lại Link Ebook: 2) Doanh nghiệp cần được đào tạo gọi vốn với mức độ chuyên nghiệp cao Hãy đăng ký "Gói BeginGuru Fundraising Coach 1vs1" Link: BeginGuru GọiVốn Startup QuảnTrịRủiRo

Content: MJF a Riccardo Donadon: un riconoscimento a chi trasforma l'innovazione in valore per la comunità. Roberto Limitone, Governatore del Distretto 108Ta3, ha conferito la Melvin Jones Fellow a Riccardo Donadon, Amministratore Delegato e fondatore di H-FARM. Un riconoscimento che premia non solo una brillante carriera imprenditoriale, capace di fare di H-FARM uno dei principali ecosistemi europei dedicati all'innovazione, all'economia digitale e alla formazione, ma anche una visione che pone la tecnologia al servizio delle persone. Attraverso il sostegno alla nascita di start-up innovative e di progetti ad alto impatto sociale, Riccardo Donadon ha saputo promuovere modelli di sviluppo fondati sulla solidarietà, sull'inclusione e sulla creazione di nuove opportunità per le giovani generazioni e per le comunità. Congratulazioni a Riccardo Donadon per questo prestigioso riconoscimento. Content: Hey fam! Last night, we joined AI Tinkerers Hong Kong & GBA at Dialogue in the Dark, a great evening about building AI that genuinely enables. 🤖 The night opened the way it should: with the people AI is meant to serve. Visually impaired hosts from Dialogue in the Dark shared how AI is already reshaping their daily lives and, just as importantly, the real-world UX problems they still need engineers to solve. A grounding reminder that accessibility isn’t a feature you bolt on at the end; it’s the brief. 🙂 From there, the demos. Three teams shared technical work spanning two accessibility projects and a live translation app - the kind of “does it actually work in the wild?” building we love to see. AWS & Kiro also dropped in for a session (and kept the room fed 🙏). What struck us most was the crowd: deeply technical, hands-on, the kind of people training their own models and hacking on hard problems for the right reasons. Exactly the energy we really love being around! 😉 At OAX Foundation, this is the intersection we care about: AI that extends human capability and reaches the people who need it most. It’s also why we back projects like mapping the people, ideas, and events moving Asia’s AI scene forward. Nights like this are a big part of that story. 🥰 Huge thanks to Dialogue in the Dark for hosting, and to AI Tinkerers HK & GBA for convening such a thoughtful room. More of this, please! 🥂 tech startup hongkong
Author: oax_foundation

Content: You know when you type a question into your LLM (let's say Claude), and it says...thinking... Or, it gives you the impression it's thinking. Well, it's not. Your AI is a predication engine. It doesn't have thoughts, or feelings for that matter. It has lots and lots of data and it uses that to predict the most likely answer. This means, it doesn't always get it right, which is why fact checking is so important. Being honest, have you started to rely on AI a bit like it's a friend? Seriously no judgement! 😊 It's easy to do, that's what tech companies are trying to achieve in their interfaces and responses. Try this instead. Use your LLM to help challenge you. To broaden your cognitive skills, not lessen them. Try sparring with your AI model on a subject you know about and whilst doing so, remember, it's a prediction engine, it's not really thinking and you can't hurt its feelings. AISolutions AIConsultant AIStrategy AutomationExpert DigitalTransformation

Content: AI can do a lot, but it can't carry responsibility for your decisions. That's why the smartest teams are building AI and human judgement side by side, on purpose. AI is great at the heavy lifting: analysing data, spotting patterns, and drafting options in seconds. Humans are great at the trust work: nuance, empathy, ethical reasoning, and accountability. So before you roll out another tool, get clear on roles. What will AI automate, what will it advise on, and what must a person approve every time? Next, build a workflow where AI outputs come with a review moment, not an auto-send button. If the decision affects people, money, safety, or privacy, add stronger oversight and clear escalation paths. Keep the loop tight so your AI improves responsibly: create, deploy, measure, review, refine, redeploy. And reinvest the time you save into the skills that compound, like critical thinking, communication, and great leadership. Our principle at Maybe* is simple: let AI banish repetitive tasks so your humans can shine at strategy and creativity. If you want a practical starting point, book a call with a Maybe* expert and we'll help you choose a safe pilot and scale with confidence. Start with one task. Head to our bio to learn more. Dán prompt bên dưới 👇 🛠 Tool: GPT Image 2 (ChatGPT Images 2.0) · Midjourney v6+ · Flux · Imagen · SDXL 📝 Prompt: Use the uploaded portrait as the exact face and identity source — preserve his real likeness, features, skin tone, natural dark hair and clear-framed glasses, no beautifying. Cinematic cyberpunk night portrait of a young Asian man on a rain-soaked city street. He wears a glossy crimson-red rain jacket (wet PU shell) over a black tee, clear-framed glasses lit by neon. Content: E SE UN'AZIENDA NON AVESSE PIÙ UN AMMINISTRATORE, MA UN'INTELLIGENZA ARTIFICIALE? In Argentina si discute una proposta di legge rivoluzionaria: riconoscere società gestite esclusivamente da sistemi di Intelligenza Artificiale ( È la domanda che si pone Yuval Noah Harari (filosofo israeliano, uno dei più grandi guru dell’IA) non è poi così banale (🔗 Sito ufficiale di Yuval Noah Harari). Oggi le aziende sono già "persone giuridiche": possono possedere beni, firmare contr tti, fare causa. Ma dietro ogni decisione c'è, almeno in teoria, una persona che ne risponde. Domani potrebbe non essere più così. Un'Intelligenza Artificiale può prendere decisioni, ottimizzare processi, gestire investimenti, negoziare contratti. Ma se sbaglia, chi ne risponde? Un algoritmo non prova senso di colpa, non teme una condanna, non ha coscienza morale. La questione, quindi, non è solo tecnologica. È giuridica, economica ed etica. Questo però non significa chiudere la porta per principio. Non dobbiamo affrontare l’Intelligenza Artificiale con paura o pregiudizio culturale. Se ben regolata, trasparente e orientata al bene comune, una nuova forma di organizzazione assistita dall’IA potrebbe anche produrre efficienza, ridurre errori, migliorare servizi e generare vantaggi reali per la società umana. Harari, come spesso accade, spinge il ragionamento fino alle conseguenze più estreme, immaginando un futuro in cui grandi organizzazioni governate dall'IA possano acquisire un potere enorme. Che questo scenario si realizzi o meno, una cosa è certa: mentre impariamo a costruire macchine sempre più intelligenti, dobbiamo imparare anche a costruire regole altrettanto intelligenti. Perché il vero problema non è che l'Intelligenza Artificiale prenda decisioni. È capire chi sarà responsabile delle loro conseguenze. 🔗 Financial Times – *We must not grant AI agents legal personhood* IntelligenzaArtificiale AI YuvalNoahHarari Harari Innovazione EticaDigitale AIGovernance Responsabilità Diritto Tecnologia FutureOfWork AgenticAI AIAgents CorporateGovernance
